General safety information
Important safety instructions
1.1 Important safety instructions
It is important to install, operate, maintain, and dispose of the equipment according to the
information in the product documentation, and in compliance with local regulations. For the
safety of the users and the patients, before a user operates or works on the equipment, they
must read, understand, and obey all:
• Warnings, cautions, instructions, and notes in this document and the related
documents.
• Safety instructions, emergency procedures, and information in this document and the
related documents.
• Warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of the
equipment.
If you have local work instructions for the equipment, then Elekta recommends that you include
the warnings, cautions, and notes from this document in your local work instructions.
1.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING 1.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of fatal or serious
injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 1.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of damage to the
equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.
1.1.2 Expected service life of the equipment
The expected service life is the length of time that the regular service and repair procedures can
keep the equipment safe to operate.
WARNING 1.2
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks, planned
maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
The expected service life of this equipment is 15 years of normal use.
The Elekta definition for 'expected service life' is the length of time that the regular service and
repair procedures can keep the equipment safe to operate.
The Elekta definition of 'normal use' is the treatment of 50 patients each day for five days each
week of the year. If the equipment is used more frequently than this, the frequency of the
planned maintenance must increase accordingly and the extended life checks must also be
brought forward accordingly.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 7

General safety information
Important safety instructions
The level of operation of the equipment can be different to ‘normal use’. This has an effect on the
length of the service life. Because of wear of parts, the risk of a malfunction can increase after 10
years operation. Elekta recommends that before you continue operation after 10 years, you
complete the regular extended life checks.
Elekta can help the User with the correct safety checks, planned maintenance schedules, and the
regular extended life checks for continued safe operation of the equipment.
The system must be maintained throughout its expected service life in compliance with the EMC
information detailed in the applicable Instructions for Use.
For information on the above checks and schedules, contact your local Elekta representative.

of, and work on, the equipment.

1.2 Safety precautions
1.2.1 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 1.3
Do not use parts, accessories, or other equipment, without approval by Elekta of their
compatibility. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the
equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of
Elekta can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the performance
of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or decrease the
immunity of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
1.2.2 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of
the electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories,
the control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other
manufacturer.
Document ID: 45133702097_13 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
Page 10 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room.
Only use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe
distance of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID
tag, must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID systems
when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING 1.4
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to the
equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC standards, and can
have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you ignore this warning, the RF
emissions can cause the equipment to have a malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury,
clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.5
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have
an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC emissions or decrease the
immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you ignore this warning, incorrect radiation
delivery and other malfunctions can occur, which can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
1.2.3 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
WARNING 1.6
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.7
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 11

General safety information
Safety precautions
• The applicable calibration certificate is valid
• The lifting equipment gives the maximum Safe Working Load (SWL).
For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the
Instructions for Use and Calibration Certificate for the tool.
1.2.4 Safety precaution for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 1.8
Do not connect any electrical power supply before the ground earth connections are secure.
There is a risk of high leakage current. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.9
Do not remove the cables or the covers, or operate the equipment without the covers. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
The covers on the equipment prevent access to high voltage parts and parts that move. If you
remove the covers, there is a risk of high voltage shock or injury from parts that move.
Only remove the covers if you are an Elekta approved person with the correct authority and you
obey the applicable safety procedures.
1.2.5 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 1.10
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, and other safety devices. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.
1.2.6 Safety precaution for work on BLDs with UV lighting
WARNING 1.11
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the BLD without protective glasses. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
The covers and screens of the BLD prevent an injury to your eyes from the ultraviolet lighting.
Before you remove the covers or screens of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator, or use
protective glasses. Protective glasses must have the UV-A specification OD 6+ @ 190 - 532 Nm.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator and contact
the Elekta representative.
Document ID: 45133702097_13 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
Page 12 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.7 Safety precaution for work with lasers
WARNING 1.12
Do not look into the laser beams because they can cause damage to your eyes. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Elekta systems use the laser beams to help set up patients in the correct position for treatment.
During your work in the treatment room, make sure that you do not look into the laser beams.
1.2.8 Safety precautions for work with display screen equipment
WARNING 1.13
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you use the display screen equipment (DSE), obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures in the jurisdiction where you do the work.
1.2.9 Safety precautions for cleaning and disinfection
WARNING 1.14
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray when the equipment is connected to
the electrical supply. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.15
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.2
Do not let water and other liquids go into the equipment. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.3
Do not use a spray to clean the medical equipment room. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room with
disinfectants. Elekta recommends that the procedures to clean the equipment and the equipment
room obey the applicable laws and regulations of the jurisdiction where the equipment is
installed.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 13

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.10 Safety precautions for work with high temperatures on the BLD
covers
During normal operation, the maximum temperatures of the accessory ring and BLD outer covers
can increase to 45 °C. Make sure that you do not touch the outer covers or the accessory ring for
more than 1 minute.
1.2.11 Safety precaution for the installation of accessories on the BLD
WARNING 1.16
Do not touch the connector found in the accessory ring on the BLD. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you install an accessory, for example, a shadow tray, electron applicator, or front pointer,
do not touch the pins in the connector. The pins have a low electrical current, which can give an
electric shock when you touch them.
1.2.12 Safety precautions for work with heavy parts
The safety precautions for work with heavy parts are the necessary safety precautions to know to
prevent injury.
WARNING 1.17
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1.2.13 Safety precautions during work on the BLD
When you do work on the BLD, make sure that you obey the following safety precautions:
WARNING 1.18
Do not do work on the inner parts of the beam limiting device while the electrical supply is
connected. There is a risk of high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.19
Do not do work on the beam limiting device unless the gantry is at 0° or 180°. Gravity can cause
the leaves, diaphragms, and dynamic leaf guides to move. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.20
Do not touch the hot LED field light projector, or the heat sink, immediately after operation. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Document ID: 45133702097_13 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
Page 14 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.14 Safety precaution for the removal of parts from the radiation head
WARNING 1.21
Do not touch the flight tube, bending magnet, or other parts near to them until a check for
induced radioactivity is completed. Excessive exposure to radioactivity is dangerous. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
After the operation of the linear accelerator at high energy, the parts in the radiation head can
become radioactive and a radiation hazard. Before you do work on, or remove parts from the
radiation head, use a calibrated radiation detector to measure the level of induced radioactivity.
Use the applicable safety procedures when you remove radioactive parts and material. The
maximum dose rate for the safe removal of radioactive parts and material is 50 µSv/hr
(microsievert per hour). Do not remove the radioactive parts and material from the treatment
room before they are correctly prepared for safe removal.
1.2.15 Safety precautions for maintenance, modification, and
decommissioning
WARNING 1.22
Do not do work, or make modifications to the equipment without the correct training and
authority. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
Only do work on, or make modifications to, the equipment if you are an Elekta approved person
with the applicable authority. Such work must use best practice and obey the laws and
regulations in the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Modifications or work that does not have approval by Elekta, and is not done by an Elekta
approved person with the correct authority, can cause:
• Fatal injury
• Incorrect radiation delivery
• Malfunction of the equipment
• Damage to the equipment
• The warranty to be canceled.
1.2.16 Safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity are the precautions to prevent
an excessive exposure to, and serious injury from, induced radioactivity.
When you operate or do work on the equipment, Elekta recommends that you use the applicable
radiation protection functions, devices, systems, procedures, and accessories. Always obey the
applicable laws and regulations for induced radioactivity for the jurisdiction in which the
equipment is installed.
The safety precautions are applicable to:
• Maintenance, modifications, and decommissioning
• Measurement of the induced radioactivity
• Removal and movement of parts with induced radioactivity.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 15

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.16.1 Safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity are the necessary safety
precautions to know to prevent excessive exposure to radioactivity, which can cause serious
injury or damage to the environment.
Always use two radiation detectors to measure the level of induced radioactivity. Only use
radiation detectors that have an applicable calibration certificate. The period of time since the
last calibration date must be less than 12 months. Do not use the radiation detectors if the period
of time since the last calibration date is more than 12 months.
WARNING 1.23
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
1.2.16.2 Safety precautions for handling parts with induced radioactivity
The handling of parts with induced radioactivity must comply with the laws and regulations of the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed. The safe levels of radiation applicable in the UK
do not apply in all jurisdictions. The permitted level of radioactivity must always be as low as
reasonably achievable (ALARA). This level may vary depending on local rules, therefore the safe
working levels from the applicable local rules must be considered as the acceptable working limit.
If there are no safe levels available for a jurisdiction, use the UK value as a guideline, which is a
maximum of 150 cps at the surface for a maximum duration of 72 minutes. The following table
shows the conversion from this value to other units.
Table 1.2 Conversion between the units of induced radioactivity
Counts per second Microsieverts per Milliroentgen per
(cps) hour (µSv/hr) hour (mR/hr)
150 50 5
For information on the safe removal of parts with induced radioactivity and the safe levels of
radiation for your jurisdiction, contact Elekta or a Qualified Expert (QE).
1.3 Emergencies
1.3.1 Safety precaution for the occurrence of fire or explosion
WARNING 1.24
Do not use the equipment in rooms that contain explosive or flammable gases. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
High levels of oxygen, and some anesthetic gases and disinfectant sprays, can make an explosive
or flammable environment in the room.
Document ID: 45133702097_13 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
Page 16 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Emergencies
1.3.2 Recommendations for emergency procedures
This is a description of the responsibilities of the User with relation to the emergency procedures
and emergency switches.
Before you use the equipment, Elekta recommends that you know all the local emergency
procedures and the locations of all the emergency switches.
The Elekta equipment includes:
• The STOP MOTORS buttons on the handheld controllers and user interface panels in
the treatment room, and FKP in the control room.
When you press these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply from the motors,
stops all movements of the equipment, and stops kV and MV radiation.
• The EMERGENCY POWER OFF buttons in the treatment room, control area, and areas
near the equipment.
When you operate these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply to the linear
accelerator. The buttons disconnect the electrical supply from many electrical circuits,
but not from all the circuits.

2.1.2 Recommendations for training
The regulations for training give a description of the necessary training, regulations, and
compliance that apply to the operation of the equipment.
Different countries have different regulations for training. Make sure that you have the necessary
training before you operate or do work on the equipment. Make sure that your training is in
compliance with the laws and regulations of the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Training is available from Elekta. Contact Elekta for more information about the applicable
training courses.
2.1.3 Disposal of the equipment
End of life (EOL) disposal is the procedure to disassemble, remove, and discard the equipment, in
full or in parts, to a condition where it is not possible to operate the equipment for its intended
use.
Only a Qualified Expert (QE) and an approved facility with an applicable license, may remove and
discard the equipment and recycle the material.
A full description of disposal requirements is detailed in a separate document Product Disposal
Information. This document is available on request from Elekta.
The customer owns the data on the storage media. If there are any data storage media such as
HDDs, remove them from the device and hand them to the customer for disposal. If the customer
entrusts the disposal team to dispose of the data, the data must be destroyed in accordance with
relevant cybersecurity regulations.

2.3 Regulatory requirements for equipment use
2.3.1 Precautions for work with electrostatic discharge sensitive devices
The precautions to prevent an electrostatic discharge (ESD) during work with ESD sensitive
devices.
CAUTION 2.1
Do not touch the PCBs and other electronic parts unless you wear a grounded antistatic wrist
strap. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 2.2
Do not touch the parts identified with the ESD caution label. Always obey the ESD precautions
during work on these parts. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
Semiconductor devices, integrated circuits, and the parts that contain them, are ESD sensitive
devices. These devices are electrostatic discharge sensitive (ESDS) devices. ESD can cause damage
to these devices and cause them to have a malfunction immediately or some time after the
electrostatic discharge. To prevent the ESD damage to these devices, always wear a grounded
antistatic wrist strap, and obey the applicable ESD precautions and procedures.
Elekta recommends that all users receive the training about ESD. The training must include the
ESD caution label and these ESD precautions.

Introduction
Document scope
3.1 Document scope
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the systems, subsystems and components of the linear accelerator. The target audience
is qualified service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified
representatives of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:
• A description of the system or sub-system in question.
• A troubleshooting section to identify the problem areas.
• A set of detailed procedures used to replace FRUs (field replaceable units).
• A section on how to return the device to work.
If you find any mistakes in this document, or identify areas for improvement, please raise a CLM
(customer lifecycle management) case and assign it to the relevant queue.

Product description of the HT and RF systems
Overview of the HT and RF systems
4.1 Overview of the HT and RF systems
This is an overview of the HT and RF systems that the linear accelerator uses to make the radiation
beam.
The primary function of a linear accelerator is to make a radiation beam of high-energy electrons
or X-rays (photons). The high tension (HT) and radio frequency (RF) systems of the linear
accelerators operate together to make the radiation beam.
The HT system makes stable, high-voltage pulses. In the RF system, the HT pulses make the
traveling RF wave which moves the electrons through a waveguide and into the beam limiting
device (BLD).
The high-voltage pulses from the HT system, energize a modulator which makes the traveling RF
wave. A magnetron in the modulator supplies a RF power input to the waveguide and therefore
makes the traveling RF wave. The HT pulses energize an electron gun which puts electrons into
the waveguide where they are moved by the RF wave. The beam physics system controls the
speed increase and the path of the electron beam.
If there are changes in the current supply to the magnetron changes, it causes the frequency and
amplitude of the RF to become less stable. The magnetron only uses a pulse for micro-seconds.
The modulator develops the high-voltage pulse.
The RF system has two basic configurations:
• RF feedback - linear accelerators with higher energies of 20 MeV/MV use the
remaining RF energy as feedback to increase the energy in the waveguide (see Figure
4.1).
• Without RF feedback - linear accelerators with a maximum energy of 18 MeV/MV
supply the remaining RF energy directly to the RF load (see Figure 4.2).

4.1.1 Description of the high tension electrical supply
This is a description of the HT electrical supply that the linear accelerator uses.
The primary power source of the linear accelerator is the 230/440 V electrical supply. The high
tension (HT) power supply unit (PSU) and the modulator together change the supply voltage into
a short pulse of between 35 kV and 50 kV in amplitude.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems

Product description of the HT and RF systems
Overview of the HT and RF systems
4.1.2 Description of the charge cycle of the high tension system
This is a description of the charge cycle of the HT system.
A switch controls a transformer which gives the storage for the supplied high tension (HT) power.
Darlington pair transistors supply this switch for linear accelerators from 105361 to 151574.
Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors supply this switch for linear accelerators from 151575.
When the switches are off, the energy held in the charge transformer is transferred to the pulse
forming network (PFN). This stays charged until the thyratron starts.
When the thyratron starts, the energy held in the PFN is discharged through the pulse
transformer. The waveform made is a negative pulse of approximately 3 µs. This pulse goes to the
cathode of the magnetron and the cathode of the electron gun. This causes the emissions of
electrons in the two devices immediately.
When the linear accelerator operates in high energy mode, two charge cycles occur before the
PFN is discharged. The two charges make a total charge of approximately 40 joules.
4.1.3 Description of the magnetron RF power supply
This is a description of how the magneton supplies the RF power for the digital accelerator.
The magnetron is installed in area 30 and makes the radio frequency (RF) power. The magnetron
has a cathode which is installed in the center of an anode block. The anode block has a number of
resonant cavities and is connected to ground (earth). An electromagnet is around the anode and
cathode.
The high tension (HT) system increases the temperature of the cathode and also supplies a pulse
to the cathode which causes the emission of electrons. The voltage between the anode and the
cathode causes a large electric field. This electric field increases the speed of the electrons in the
direction of the anode. This makes an “electron cloud”, which increases and oscillates in the
anode at the resonant frequency of the magnetron, which aligns to that of the waveguide system.
The effect of the resonant cavities of the anode change the energy of the accelerating electrons
to RF energy. A slot in the anode supplies the RF power from the magnetron to the waveguide
system.
An automatic frequency control (AFC) system keeps the accurate control of the resonant
frequency of the magnetron. It does this by the adjustment of the dimensions of the cavities. This
is known as magnetron tuning.
The small adjustment of the current to the magnetron magnet controls the intensity of the
magnetic field around the magnetron.
The power supply that increases the temperature of the cathode, has a small adjustment to keep
the cathode at the correct temperature. This stops the temperature increase that can occur when
some of the electrons go back to the cathode.
4.1.3.1 Description of automatic frequency control
This is a description of the AFC system which tunes the magnetron frequency.
The AFC system is a RF phase error detector that tunes the resonant frequency of the magnetron.
The AFC system compares the phase of the source RF with a RF sample from the middle of the
waveguide. To compare the two phases, the AFC system reads sample from the middle of the
waveguide after approximately 350 ns. Therefore, if the temperature changes in the magnetron
and the waveguide, the phase of the RF waves stays constant.
Document ID: 45133702097_13 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
Page 40 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

Product description of the HT and RF systems
Overview of the HT and RF systems
4.1.4 Description of the electron gun - electron generation
This is a description of the electron gun.
The source of electrons is the electron gun. The cathode releases the electrons when it gets a
pulse from the HT system.
The anode of the electron gun makes the electrons focus into a small beam. The electrons then
go through a hole in the anode into the accelerating waveguide.
The filament of the cathode has a heater. Therefore a small adjustment of the power supply to
the filament heater controls the amplitude of the electron gun output (the beam current).
4.1.5 Description of the particle acceleration
This is a description of the particle acceleration of the digital accelerator.
The waveguide increases the velocity of the electrons from their input energy of 38 kV to 52 kV to
their last energy of 4 MeV to 25 MeV.
Electron energy comes from the electromagnetic fields caused as RF waves in the linear
accelerator. The electrons travel with the RF waves, and they acquire energy continuously from
the axial electric field part of the waves.
4.1.6 Description of the RF feedback system
This is a description of the RF feedback system which maximizes the energy supply to the
waveguide in high power linear accelerators.
The current of the electron beam changes as the energy that is necessary changes. In the Electron
mode, the current is in mA. The X-ray mode has higher currents. Therefore, in X-ray mode, a small
quantity of RF power stays in the waves at the waveguide output. In Electron mode, a large
quantity of RF power stays in the waves at the waveguide output.
In high power machines, the RF system has a feedback system that stays at the waveguide output
again. The directional coupler collects the remaining RF power together and applies it near the
input of the waveguide system. This supply mixes with the RF input to the magnetron which
increases the power flux into the linear accelerator.
4.1.6.1 Description of the remaining RF phase control
This is a description of the remaining RF phase control.
A high power phase (HP) shifter, in the feedback waveguide loop, sets the correct phase of the
remaining RF power to be mixed. The correct frequency of the magnetron output and the correct
setting of the phase shifter are set automatically in software when the mode of operation is
selected. In low power machines, the RF load absorbs the unwanted RF power.
4.1.7 Description of the gas and vacuum separation
This is a description of the gas and vacuum separation.
The rectangular waveguide system (which includes the isolator, the directional coupler, and the
phase shifter) is filled with dielectric gas (SF6) at low pressure (0.8 bar). This is isolated from the
high vacuum accelerator waveguide by two waveguide windows in the input and the output
rectangular waveguide supplies.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 41

Product description of the HT and RF systems
Overview of the HT and RF systems
4.1.8 Description of the electric field mode transformation
This is a description of the electric field mode transformation.
The input mode transformer and the output mode transformer are side wall couplers that
connect the rectangular waveguide to the waveguide.
The transformers let the full flow of RF power with negligible reflection. The RF field is changed
from transverse electric (TE) to axial electric at the input, and the other way around at the output.
The electric and magnetic fields are at right angles to each other therefore, magnetically, the
change is from axial magnetic at the input to transverse magnetic (TM) at the output.
4.1.9 Description of the high energy beams
This is a description of the high energy beams.
The selection of a high energy treatment decreases the pulse repetition frequency (PRF) by half.
The charge cycle continues at the same rate, therefore in high energy mode, there are two charge
cycles for each discharge cycle. This increases the PFN voltage from 14 kV to 19 kV.
4.1.10 Description of the protection against reverse voltage
This is a description of the protection against reverse voltage.
If the discharge cycle does not agree, this causes an opposite voltage across the pulse forming
network (PFN). A diode and a number of resistors send the opposite voltage to earth. A voltage is
developed across the last resistor in the array, and this voltage supplied to a diode pump. A
comparator then compares the pump voltage to a level set by the comparator. When the
comparator senses six pulses in sequence, it will release.
The output from the comparator prevent the charge enable signals and stop the charge of the
PFN. The inhibit is for 1.5 seconds and is known as the reverse diode overload.
4.1.11 Description of the HT PSU
This is a description of the HT PSU which supplies power to the RF system.
The high tension (HT) power supply unit (PSU) is a switched mode type. A software HT drive pulse
controls the power to the radio frequency (RF) system.
The HT PSU has two modes of operation:
Low energy In the low energy mode, the power supply supplies one pulse
charge of approximately 20 joules to the pulse forming network
(PFN).
High energy In the high energy mode, the power supply supplies two
charges in sequence of approximately 20 joules. This gives a
total charge of approximately 40 joules.
An analog signal Item 330 controls the level of the energy. This shows the rate of the charge.
The HT PSU operates at the same pulse repetition rate as the magnetron. A pulse of
approximately 2.4 ms starts the charge before the magnetron pulse occurs.
Document ID: 45133702097_13 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
Page 42 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

Product description of the HT and RF systems
Description of the RF system
4.2 Description of the RF system
This is a description of the RF system.
The RF system moves the RF power from the magnetron through the waveguide to the BLD.
The system contains the magnetron, the electron gun, and a waveguide assembly. The waveguide
increases the velocity of the electrons from their input energy to their last energy.
4.2.1 Description of the waveguide assembly
4.2.1.1 Description of the input part of the waveguide
This is a description of the input part of the waveguide of the digital accelerator.
The RF wave from the magnetron goes through a section of rectangular waveguide before it goes
into the accelerating waveguide. The rectangular waveguide section has a RF isolator, which stops
the accumulation of the standing waves. In linear accelerators with RF feedback, the rectangular
section also has a sidewall coupler, which sends part of the RF that is not used, to the moving
wave.
A section of flexible waveguide connects the rectangular section to a RF input window that keeps
the gas filled rectangular waveguide from the evacuated accelerating waveguide.
4.2.1.2 Description of the accelerating waveguide
This is a description of the part of the waveguide that accelerates the electrons.
This part of the waveguide has two sections that are in a cylinder shape. These are connected at
the center. A mode transformer is at each end of the waveguide. Each of the waveguide sections
has a number of tuned resonant cavities. These cavities, together with a strong magnetic field,
adds the necessary velocity to the electrons.
The waveguide is an evacuated vacuum to prevent the blockage or the travel of the electrons by
collisions with the gas molecules. The vacuum is sealed at the two mode transformers.
Water keeps this part of the waveguide cool, therefore the waveguide stays in tune.
4.2.1.3 Description of the feedback section of the rectangular waveguide
This is a description of the feedback section of the rectangular waveguide.
On high energy linear accelerators (with feedback), the remaining RF energy is moved around and
added to the input power. This increases the speed of the waveguide.
4.2.2 Description of the mode transformers
This is description of the mode transformers which align the RF energy in the waveguide with the
electrons that are emitted from the electron gun.
The RF that is input to the accelerating waveguide moves at a velocity that is too high to align
with the electrons from the gun. The input mode transformer changes the Field Mode of the
wave from transverse electric (TE) to transverse magnetic (TM).
At the output end of the accelerating waveguide, the output mode transformer restores the TE
Field Mode.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 43

Product description of the HT and RF systems
Description of the HT and RF system control
4.2.3 Description of the RF probes
This is a description of the RF probes which are installed in the accelerating waveguide.
The input RF window has a RF probe that supplies one of two sample signals for the phase error
detector of the automatic frequency control (AFC) system. The center joint of the accelerating
waveguide has a second RF probe that gives the other sample signal to the phase error detector.
4.3 Description of the HT and RF system control
This is a description of the HT and RF system control.
The TCS software and monitor items control the HT and the RF systems.
The HTCA includes the PCBs that follow:
• Programmable pulse generator (PPG) PCB - which controls the charge of the HT
system, the current for the electron gun filament and the magnetron tuner, RF phase
sample and the AFC system.
• HT control (HTC) PCB - which controls the quantity of charge that the HT PSU gives to
the PFN. This PCB also has the protection circuit.
• Thyratron pulser unit - which controls the pulse of the thyratron and therefore the
discharge of the PFN.
• Magnetron tuner/gun filament (MT/GF) drive interface PCB - which gives the drive
signals from the PPG PCB to the UPD PCBs.
• HT PSU driver PCBs or HT PSU trigger PCB - which controls the power to the switch
devices.
Note: HT PSU driver PCBs are used with the Darlington pair transistors (on machine number range from
105361 to 151574) and the new HT PSU trigger PCB is used with IGBTs (on machine numbers
151575 forwards).
• Universal power drive (UPD) PCBs - these are in two pairs. One pair gives the
magnetron tune solenoid current and the other pair supplies the electron gun filament
current.
• Programmable PSU - which gives the magnetron filament current.
• HTCA optical receiver - which gives the isolate signals interchanged between the PCBs
of the HT and RF systems.
• HT crowbar detector PCB- this gives the protection against fault conditions.
• Modulator isolation PCB - which conditions PCB signal, LED indication of HT faults, and
monitor test points.
• HT isolation unit - which gives optical isolation for the protection circuit signals from
the HT crowbar detector PCB and the charging transformer.
To give the control of the HT and RF systems, the TCS sends a large number of control items to the
systems, and receives many monitor items. The TCS monitors and controls:
• HT charge
• Magnetron magnet current
• Magnetron tuning and AFC
• Magnetron filament current
• Electron gun filament current

4.4 Description of the service panel
This is a description of the service panel that gives test points for the HT and RF systems.
The service panel gives access to points that let you do a test of the function of the HT and RF
systems. The service panel is installed in the control room of the linear accelerator.
Refer to Troubleshooting for the RF and HT systems for more information.
4.5 System description of the HT system
4.5.1 Overview of the HT system
This is a overview of the HT system of the digital accelerator.
The high tension (HT) system changes the three-phase primary electrical supply into high power
pulses that operate the magnetron and the electron gun in the radio frequency (RF) system.
The HT system has three primary parts, which are the:
• HTCA
• HT PSU
• Modulator assembly.
The high tension control area (HTCA) contains the PCBs that the treatment control system (TCS)
uses to control the HT system. Signals from the HTCA to the TCS show the necessary adjustments.
The signals also show when it is necessary to shut down the system if there is a malfunction.
The HT PSU contains the:
• Primary input filter and rectifier
• Switch devices and their driver PCBs
• Charging transformer
• Crowbar detector PCB.
In linear accelerators that use the Darlington pairs, each of the two transistors has a driver PCB. In
linear accelerators that use the Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors, one trigger PCB operates the
two Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors.
The modulator assembly contains the:
• Thyratron
• Pulse transformer
• PFN
• Magnetron
• Other parts of the HT and RF systems.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13

Product description of the HT and RF systems
System description of the HT system
4.5.1.1 Description of the primary parts of the HT system
This is a description of the primary parts of the HT system.
The primary parts of the high tension (HT) system are found in a number of areas of the linear
accelerator. The HT system has:
• HTCA
• HT PSU chassis assembly
• Modulator assembly
• HT interlock system.
4.5.2 Operation of the HT system
This is a description of the operation of the HT system.
The high tension (HT) power supply unit (PSU) is the source of the high voltage that charges the
pulse forming network (PFN). The PSU charges the charging transformer with a controlled
quantity of energy, and then transfers the energy to the PFN (see Figure 4.3).
NOT TO SCALE 004420 ©2013 Elekta Limited
Figure 4.3 Schematic diagram of the HT system
A three-phase, full wave bridge rectifier and smoothing circuit gives approximately 600 VDC
directly from the primary supply. When the switch devices pair are switched on, this applies
600 VDC to the primary winding of the charging transformer. This causes the current to increase
linearly in the primary winding by di = V/L.dt. During this period, the voltage across the
secondary winding of the charging transformer is negative and the current flow is blocked by
diodes in the transformer.
The energy kept in the transformer is in proportion to the square of the current. The switch
devices are switched off at a set value of current to accurately control the quantity of energy kept
in the transformer.
When the switch devices are switched off, the energy causes the voltage across the secondary
winding of the charging transformer to become positive. The current that results is in a cosine
waveform, and goes through the diodes to the PFN. When all of the energy is transferred to the
PFN, the secondary voltage falls. This reverse biases the diodes again and the PFN stays charged.
For a charge of 20 joules, the primary current switches off at approximately 160 A.
When the thyratron is started, the energy kept in the pulse forming network (PFN) is discharged
through the primary winding of the pulse transformer. The waveform travels to the opposite end
of the PFN and is sent back. The time for this change gives the length of the pulse (approximately
Document ID: 45133702097_13 Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
Page 46 © Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved.

Product description of the HT and RF systems
System description of the HT system
3 µs). The negative pulse at the free terminal of the PFN is connected to the primary winding of
the pulse transformer to complete the discharge circuit.
When the linear accelerator operates in high energy mode, two charge cycles occur before the
PFN is discharged. The two charges give a total charge of approximately 40 joules.
4.5.2.1 Operation of the primary electrical supply
This is a description of the operation of the primary electrical supply.
The three-phase primary electrical supply to the HT system is connected through circuit breaker
CB1 and a number of contactors in series. Each of the contactors has a dedicated interlock chain
that controls the contactor operation.
When all the interlocks operate correctly and CB1 is ON, the primary electrical supply goes to the
high tension (HT) PSU assembly.
4.5.2.2 Operation of the HT charge cycle
This is a description of the operation of the HT system charge cycle.
The high tension (HT) PSU supplies the input power to the modulator. The pulse transformer and
the pulse forming network (PFN) in the modulator supply the HT pulses that operate the cathode
of the magnetron and the cathode of the electron gun.
The HT PSU and the modulator together give a short pulse of between 35 kV and 50 kV. This is
through a number of energy transfer steps (see Figure 4.4).
NOT TO SCALE 004419 ©2013 Elekta Limited
Figure 4.4 HT and RF charge cycle
Step Description
1 A rectifier changes the three-phase primary input electrical supply to DC. This is
then input to a number of electrolytic capacitors in the HT PSU for storage.
2 When the switches are on, energy is transferred from the 600 V capacitors and
input to the charge transformer for storage. A control circuit turns the switches
off when the current in the primary coil of the charge transformer is at a set
level. At this point, the energy for the active charge cycle is defined.
A current transformer (T4) measures the set level: the TCS refers to this level as
the primary current monitor.
The primary charge phase is between 200 µs to 700 µs. The current in the
primary coil of the charge transformer is in proportion to the charge phase time
(energy = ½LI2).
3 With the switches off, current cannot flow in the primary coil of the charge
transformer. This causes the current to go back(flyback) in the secondary coil.
The diode at the secondary coil lets current through, and the energy in the core
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 47

Product description of the HT and RF systems
System description of the HT system
Step Description
of the charge transformer flows into the PFN. The PFN has a number of
capacitors which give the storage.
The voltage across the secondary coil of the charge transformer (21 kV) is much
higher than that of the primary coil (12 kV). Secondary energy transfer occurs
0.8 µs to 1.5 µs after the switches are off.
4 When the voltage across the PFN has become stable, the thyratron operates to
supply the high 3 µs pulses. These pulses first drive the primary coils of the
pulse transformer and then the anode of the magnetron.
During the discharge phase, the PFN operates as an applied transmission line
(rather than a simple capacitor). This is because the PFN is a network of
capacitors and inductors.
In high power mode, two charge cycles occur before the thyratron starts. This
gives a higher PFN voltage.
After the operation of the thyratron, the modulator operates as a transmission line that has a
primary impedance of 8 Ω and a secondary impedance of 225 Ω. The PFN supplies half of its open
circuit voltage into a load (that is a 20 kV PFN charge results in 10 kV on the primary of the
transformer, or 53 kV on the secondary because of the 5.3:1 step up ratio).
The magnetic field strength gives the magnetron voltage and the modulator adjusts the
magnetron current. If there is an important variation in the operation of the magnetron, away
from 225Ω, gives a mismatch of the modulator. If the variation is too high, the energy is
transferred to the PFN.
The modulator also supplies HT to the electron gun. The gun resistor, and the of the gun
transformer and HT cables, give a RC circuit. This gives an interval in the voltage increase time at
the gun to the magnetron pulse. The interval gives time for the RF to become stable in the
waveguide before the electrons are put in the waveguide.
4.5.2.3 Description of the HT protection circuits
This is a description of the HT protection circuits which give the HT system protection against a
number of fault conditions.
The HT protection circuits give the HT system protection against a number of fault conditions. The
fault conditions include:
• Unwanted signals
• Too much current
• Too much voltage
• Reverse diode overload
• High temperature.
If one or more of these fault conditions occur, the HTC PCB does not send the HT drive pulse to
the HT circuit.
4.5.3 Description of the HT control area
This is a description of the HT control area (HTCA) in Area 16 of the gantry drum of the linear
accelerator.
The high tension control area (HTCA) contains the PCBs that control the operation of the high
tension (HT) systems. Other PCBs that are used in other control areas of the linear accelerator are
also installed in the HTCA.

4.5.4.1 Description of the three-phase primary filter
This is a description of the three-phase primary filter.
The three-phase primary input filter isolates the primary electrical supply from noise from the
switch circuits. It is a filter unit with two capacitors to increase the noise performance. The three-
phase output lines from the filter are applied to the rectifier on the heat sink assembly.
4.5.4.2 Description and operation of the heat sink assembly
This is a description of the operation of the heat sink assembly for linear accelerators from serial
number 151575.
The heatsink removes heat from the HT PSU and also has a primary supply rectifier. The heatsink
is in area 17 of the linear accelerator. The heatsink has the parts that follow:
• Cooling fan
• Three-phase mains rectifier (three diode pairs)
• One mains transformer for the HT PSU trigger PCB
• One HT PSU IGBT trigger PCB and power supply transformer—on the chassis
• Two IGBTs (TR1 and TR2)
• Current transformer
• A thermal switch which operates if it senses that the temperature is too high
• A busbar for connections.
The heat sink assembly has power devices installed on three of the outer faces (see Figure 4.8).

Item Description
Mains rectifier The three-phase output of the primary electrical supply filter is supplied to a

resistors, connected across the capacitors, make sure that the capacitors
discharge after operation. It is approximately 12 minutes for the capacitors
to discharge to a safe level.
IGBT Two IGBT devices (TR1 and TR2) control the current flow through the primary
winding of the charge transformer. The IGBTs switch 600 V DC to the charge
transformer at approximately -300 V and +300 V to the primary electrical
supply neutral connection.
The +300 V is connected to C1 TR1 and C1 TR2 (that is, anode (collector) of
TR1 and TR2). The -300 V is connected to E2 TR1 and E2 TR2 (that is, cathode
(emitter) of TR1 and TR2).
The primary winding of the charge transformer is connected across the
output of the IGBTs. The outputs are C2E1 TR1 and C2E1 TR2.
There are two IGBTs in each module, only one is used in each. The modules
are therefore operated from the IGBT trigger PCB in half bridge mode. TR1
through G1 E1 and TR2 through G2 E2.
The operation of the IGBT trigger PCB causes the transformer to start to
charge. This causes active devices of TR1 and TR2 to operate and therefore
the current in the primary winding of the charge transformer starts to
increase. The active IGBTs are switched off by the IGBT trigger PCB when the
current in the primary winding is more than the rate of the charge (Item 330)
as set by the TCS, . This transfers the charge to the PFN.
Current When IGBTs TR1 and TR2 operate, the current in the primary winding of the
transformer charge transformer starts to increase. The current transformer T4 on the HT
PSU heat sink assembly monitors the current, and gives a voltage which is in
proportion to the primary current. The output of transformer T4 is current
monitor signal PRI I MON that is supplied to the HTC PCB through a coaxial
cable.

with a 0.5 A fuse in the unit.

4.5.4.5 Operation of the HT crowbar detector PCB
This is a description of the operation of the HT crowbar detector PCB.
The HT crowbar detector PCB is installed on the HT PSU chassis assembly. The HT crowbar
detector PCB senses voltages on the secondary winding of the charging transformer. If it senses a
voltage that is too high, it sends a signal to stop the charge current.
A tap on the secondary winding of the charging transformer supplies a voltage to the HT crowbar
detector PCB. If the voltage is too high, this starts a pair of thyristor switches. The thyristors cause
the power held in the charging transformer to be put into load resistor R2 on the HT PSU chassis
assembly. When this occurs, a signal is sent to the crowbar timer on the HTC PCB to prevent more
charges for a period of 1.5 seconds.
See the Linear Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Reference Manual for information about the
input and output signals to and from the HT crowbar detector PCB.
4.5.4.6 Operation of the crowbar power dump resistor
This is a description of the operation of the crowbar power dump resistor.
The HT PSU has a resistor which removes the power held in the charging transformer if the HT
crowbar detector PCB operates.
4.5.4.7 Operation of the power smoothing choke
This is a description of the operation of the power smoothing choke.
The HT PSU has a choke that gives the protection to the rectifiers and the primary electrical
supply from the high current pulses supplied by the switch circuits.
4.5.4.8 Operation of the power capacitors
This is a description of the operation of the power capacitors.
The HT PSU has four power capacitors that hold the DC primary electrical supply and usually
charge to the peak mains voltage. Two capacitors are connected in series to get the necessary

4.5.4.9 Operation of the charging transformer
This is a description of the operation of the charging transformer.
The charging transformer gives the HT CHARGE signal to the PFN in the modulator assembly.
The switch devices TR1 and TR2 are connected to the primary winding at terminals P2 and P1.
When the devices operate, they give approximately -300 V and +300 V in relation to the neutral
connection. This enables the current flow through the primary winding. This makes a high flux
density in the core of the transformer.
When the switch devices TR1 and TR2 are switched off, the current flow in the primary winding
stops which closes the magnetic field . This changes the direction of the voltage across the
secondary winding of the charging transformer to cause the high voltage charge.
The HT CHARGE signal is applied to the modulator assembly from terminal S3 through a coaxial
cable. The secondary return at terminal S1 and the pulse ground (earth) at terminal S2 are
applied to the HT crowbar detector PCB.
The charging transformer assembly is water-cooled.
4.5.5 Operation of the HT parts of the modulator assembly
This is a description of the operation of the HT parts of the modulator assembly
The modulator assembly is in area 30 of the linear accelerator. The assembly has a frame in which
a number of parts of the HT system are installed, refer to the Elekta Oncology Service Parts
Catalog for more information.
See the Linear Accelerator System Diagrams Manual for the circuit diagram of the modulator
assembly.
The HT parts of the modulator assembly has the parts that follow:
• Thyratron
• Thyratron pulser unit
• Pulse forming network
• Pulse transformer
• Reverse diode overload detector.
The modulator assembly also contains the magnetron, the modulator isolation unit, the electron
gun, and other parts of the RF system.
4.5.5.1 Operation of the thyratron
This is a description of the operation of the thyratron.
The thyratron discharges the PFN through the pulse transformer. The device is a hydrogen filled
tetrode with a ceramic envelope and two grids (G1 and G2) between the cathode and the anode.
The thyratron is a very fast, high current switch, that uses the hydrogen gas as the switch
medium. The hydrogen gas is ionized to make a conductive plasma. The temperature of the
cathode is then increased to free the electrons. Grid G1 is positive to attract the electrons which
ionizes the hydrogen. The grid 1 voltage decreases when there is plasma and current flows
between the cathode and grid 1.

System description of the HT system
In the off condition, the thyratron has a high voltage. In the on condition after start the thyratron
has a high peak current. Then, by ion recombination, the device goes quickly to the off condition.
This quality gives a high fire repetition rates (seeFigure 4.10 ).
+900 V dc
NOT TO SCALE 004433 ©2013 Elekta Limited
Figure 4.10 Thyratron
Item Description
Cathode heater A 6.3 V AC supply increases the temperature of the cathode and
releases the electrons.
Ionization voltage Grid G1 receives a +140 V DC supply which operates the hydrogen
ionization. As the hydrogen ionization occurs, the voltage measured
on grid 1 decreases to a level of between 12 V and 22 V.
Hold off grid When the PFN charges, there is a high voltage between the anode
and the cathode. Grid G2 is therefore held negative (-140 V) to
isolate the plasma from the high PFN voltage on the anode. This
prevents current flow between the cathode and the anode.
Grid G2 receives a large positive pulse of +900 V DC for 3 µs, which
causes the plasma to expand in the envelope. This causes the
thyratron to operate momentarily.
When the PFN is fully discharged, the voltage between the anode
and the cathode is negligible. The operation of the thyratron stops
and is switched off, to prepare for the next charge cycle.
Replenisher The hydrogen gas is kept as titanium hydride in metal containers in
the valve, and is filled by heating the containers. The input to the
replenisher is the 5.5 V AC power supply, which is accurately set to
release the correct gas pressure for the operation of the device.
As the thyratron ages, the gas pressure decreases and causes the G1
voltage to increase. The gas pressure can be increased if the
replenisher heater voltage is increased.

Item Description
Cooling The thyratron handles currents to a maximum of 4000 A and is
therefore air cooled by a fan.
Thyratron life In usual operation, the hydrogen in the valve envelope gradually
diffuses to air, therefore the life of a thyratron usually ends because
of the depletion of hydrogen. However, if the replenisher voltage is
too high, the hydrogen pressure is too high also and the device may
spontaneously conduct (known as fire through). If the replenisher
voltage is too low, damage to the anode may result, which also
shortens the life of the valve. It is therefore essential that the
thyratron is set to operate under the correct conditions.

4.5.5.3 Operation of the thyratron pulser unit power supplies
This is a description of the operation of the power supplies for the thyratron pulser unit.
The primary winding of transformer T1 in the thyratron pulser unit is connected at PL32A to the
220/240 V AC primary electrical supply from the HT isolation unit. The secondary winding of T1
gives ±100 V for supply to a rectifier in thyratron trigger PCB, which supplies the ±140 V DC rails in
the PCB. The ±100 V lines are fused (FS32F and FS32G) at 250 mA.
The primary winding of transformer T2 connects to the primary electrical supply. The secondary
winding of T2 gives the 5.5 V AC supply to the heater of the hydrogen replenisher. The 220/240 V
line is fused (FS32C) at 500 mA.
The primary winding of transformer T3 is connected to the primary electrical supply. The
secondary winding of T3 gives the 6.3 V AC supply to the cathode heater. The 220/240 V line is
fused (CB32D) at 1.5 A.
The primary electrical 220/240 V supplies the cooling fan of the thyratron, this is fused (FS32B) at
500 mA.

This is a description of the operation of the thyratron trigger PCB.
See the Linear Accelerator System Diagrams Manual for the circuit diagram of the thyratron
trigger PCB.
The thyratron trigger PCB sends the pulses that control the thyratron in when it receives the
thyratron trigger signal from the PPG PCB.
The active high THY_TRIG signal from the PPG PCB is input at socket SK1. The trigger signal is
opto-coupled (IC1 and IC3), inverted (IC2), and applied to a MOSFET switch (TR1). The MOSFET

A sample of the G2 signal at connector SK2 is applied, via the drum wiring, to BNC connector DD

4.5.5.5 Operation of the pulse forming network
This is a description of the operation of the pulse forming network.
See the Linear Accelerator System Diagrams Manual.
The PFN circuit controls the shape and length of the voltage input to the pulse transformer. The
pulse forming network (PFN) has eight LC circuits contained in an assembly installed on the
modulator assembly.
The HT CHARGE signal from the charging transformer in the HT PSU is supplied to terminal T1 of
the PFN. The free end of the PFN at terminal T0 is connected to the primary winding of the pulse
transformer.
When enabled, the HT CHARGE signal charges the PFN to between 14 kV to 19 kV
(approximately). When the thyratron starts, the PFN discharges through the pulse transformer.
4.5.5.6 Operation of the pulse transformer
This is a description of the operation of the pulse transformer.
See the Linear Accelerator System Diagrams Manual.
The pulse transformer T1 supplies the high voltage pulses to the cathode of the magnetron and
the cathode of the electron gun. The transformer has a voltage step-up ratio of 1:5.3. This gives
an output of approximately 52 kV in the high power mode and 38 kV in the low power mode.
The pulse from the PFN is supplied to connector P2 and is input to the positive end of the primary
winding. The output from the secondary winding is a pulse at terminal S4 and is input to the
magnetron and the electron gun through coaxial cables.
When loaded with the magnetron impedance across the secondary winding, the primary winding
of the pulse transformer has an impedance that is approximately equal to the impedance (Z ) of
o
the PFN (Zo = 8 Ω). Voltage VN is therefore shared equally between Z and the reflected
o
impedance (for example, VN = 21 kV gives 10.4 kV across the primary winding).
When the voltage is at 80% of the full pulse level, the magnetron gives current (see Figure 4.12).
To get the correct rate of the voltage increase and make sure that the magnetron starts in the
correct mode, an array of capacitors (C3 to C10) is connected across the secondary winding of the
pulse transformer. The capacitance of the array mixes with the capacitance and leakage
inductance of the pulse transformer to give the rate of magnetron voltage increase (110 kV/µs).

4.6.4 Operation of the modulator isolation unit PCB
This is a description of the operation of the modulator isolation PCB.
The modulator isolation PCB conditions some signals between the units of the HT PSU and give
LED indication of HT faults. The modulator isolation PCB is installed in Area 34.
The PCB includes the reverse diode overload detector.
Item Description
Modulator The MODULATOR O/T signal from the temperature sensor in the
overtemperature pulse transformer illuminates the fiber-optic connection OPTO 2 if
the system senses a high temperature. This circuit also gives the
active high MODULATOR OVERTEMP signal to the HTC PCB.
Thyratron drive The PPG PCB supplies a THYRATRON DRIVE PULSE. The HT isolation
unit applies this to the modulator isolation unit.
Thyratron grid G1 monitor The modulator isolation unit receives signal THYRATRON G1 MON
from the thyratron trigger PCB. The level of this signal shows the
bias voltage at grid G1 of the thyratron.
The signal is applied to the negative input of a voltage comparator
(IC1). The positive input is set by the divider network R4 and R5 to
make a level detector. TR1 inverts the output of the level detector to
give the THYRATRON G1 MON signal to the HTCA optical receiver.
The THYRATRON G1 MON signal is also applied to a level detector
(IC2). This illuminates LED OPTO 3 when the voltage at grid G1 of the
thyratron is less than 22 V. When illuminated, the LED shows that
the thyratron is prepared to operate, which must occur before the
modulator can operate.
PFN voltage monitor The PFN voltage monitor circuit prevents too high a charge of the
PFN. The PFN voltage is monitored to stop the charge when the
voltage increases to a set level. The linear accelerator operates at
two energies, therefore there are two level detectors in the
modulator isolation unit. They monitor different voltage levels for
the high energy and low energy modes.

4.6.5 Operation of the diode overload detector
This is a description of the operation of the diode overload detector circuit.
This circuit gives the modulator and the magnetron protection from an electrical arc and short
circuits. If these conditions occur, they can send the energy to the PFN.
An electrical arc can occur in the magnetron, usually when first used after a long standby period.
The magnetron is not damaged by an electrical arc, but it is necessary to know the quantity of
energy that goes through the diode overload circuit. This is because the circuit cannot remove the
power continuously.
It is therefore necessary to know the number of times that the diode operates. The reverse diode
overload detector knows when a specified number of in sequence of opposite energy occurs. The
number of changes in direction are as follows:
• 12 in sequence full changes of direction in the high energy mode
• 24 in sequence full changes of direction in the low energy mode.
The voltage of signal HT CHARGEmonitors the diode current. This is developed across resistor R5
in series with the diode D1. This voltage is a negative pulse which decreases very quickly. The
pulses, signal REVERSE DIODE O/L, are then supplied to connector P6 on the modulator isolation
unit.
At the modulator isolation unit, the REVERSE DIODE O/L signal is applied to a diode circuit that
has D5, R18 and C7. Each pulse charges C7 which increase its voltage in steps in a negative
direction. The voltage across C7 is then applied to detector IC3 which senses the level. IC3
operates at -10 V set by resistors R20 and R21.
When the voltage across C7 is less than 10 V, the output of IC3 is low and fiber-optic connection
OPTO 4 is illuminated. When the voltage across C7 is more than 10 V, the output of IC3 goes high.
This switches off the output of OPTO 4 and at the same time discharges C7 through diode D6 and
resistor R23.
The fiber-optic from the diode overload detector is inverted to give the active high REVERSE
DIODE O/L input to the HTC PCB.
The reverse diode overload detector gives the PFN protection. The HT CHARGE signal develops a
voltage at resistor R1. The voltage is applied to the modulator isolation unit as signal PFN V MON.

4.6.6 Operation of the gun filament transformer
This is a description of the operation of the gun filament transformer.
The gun filament transformer isolates the 50 kV supply between the gun filament power supply
circuit and the electron gun. The gun filament transformer is installed in the modulator Area 30.
The magnetron tuner/gun filament filter supplies the primary winding of the transformer. The
secondary winding of the transformer supply the output to the cathode of the electron gun. A
resistor (R6) puts a short interval in the pulse. To decrease the primary current, the gun filament
transformer has a step down ratio of 3:1.
4.6.7 Operation of the magnetron tuner gun filament filter
This is a description of the operation of the magnetron tuner/gun filament filter.
The magnetron tuner and gun filament use an EMC filter to give the protection against noise. The
EMC filter is installed in the FTM rack.
The magnetron tuner drive signals from the two magnetron tuner UPD PCBs are applied to the
filter and then to the solenoid of the magnetron tuner.
The gun filament drive signals from the two gun filament UPD PCBs are applied to the high
electrical current filter and then to the gun transformer.
4.6.8 Operation of the magnetron
This is a description of the operation of the magnetron.
The magnetron is a high power oscillator that changes high voltage pulses at its cathode into a
pulse of RF power. The magnetic field intensity and the amplitude of the voltage at the cathode
give the level of the RF output. The RF output is then input to the waveguide system.
The magnetron has a peak output of 5 MW. It operates at a nominal frequency of 2856 MHz. A
servo control system tunes the magnetron to the frequency that is necessary.
The magnetron is water cooled and evacuated, and the magnetron magnet is water cooled. The
RF power goes through an output window to the waveguide. This part of the waveguide is kept
cool by the SF gas.
6
4.6.8.1 Operation of the magnetron cathode and filament
This is a description of the operation of the magnetron cathode and filament in the digital
accelerator.
The cathode has a hot spiral filament that operates at a temperature of approximately 840 °C.
The two terminals of the filament are attached to the cathode.
The magnetron cathode has negative pulses with voltages in the range 38 kV to 52 kV from the
pulse transformer in the modulator. This gives pulse currents to a maximum of 250 A. This makes
a voltage between the cathode and the ground (earth) anode . This gives the large electric field
that is necessary to make the electrons go to the anode.
A servo controlled power supply increases the temperature of the filament. This gives the small
adjustment of the current through the filament to keep the correct temperature. Control of the
filament temperature is necessary to remove the effects of back bombardment when the
magnetron is in operation.

4.6.8.2 Operation of the magnetron anode
This is a description of the operation of the magnetron anode.
The anode block is a cylinder that has a number of vanes that are configured to make 12 different
resonant cavities. Six of the 12 cavities have a prong that moves in and out of the cavity. The six
prongs move together, operated by a fast moving magnet in a fixed solenoid. The position of the
prongs changes the physical dimensions of the cavities, and therefore adjusts the frequency at
which the magnetron operates.
The case of the anode is connected to ground (earth) to supply a high voltage between the anode
and the cathode.
4.6.8.3 Operation of the magnetron tuning
This is a description of the operation of the magnetron tuning.
In Auto mode, the PPG PCB keeps the frequency at which the magnetron is tuned. This is in
response to the RF phase data from the phase error detector of the AFC system. The TCS applies
signals which control a pair of UPD PCBs that operate the magnet of the tuning solenoid. The
current through the solenoid gives the actual position of the solenoid magnet. The current is
monitored, and this data is sent back to the TCS as a monitor item. The TCS controls the
magnetron tuning when the control software is in Manual mode only, therefore the servo control
loop does not use the monitor signal.
When an energy is selected at the start of a treatment, the low power (LP) phase shifter of the
AFC system uses the phase error detector. This sets the RF phase error to zero.
4.6.8.4 Operation of the magnetron magnet
This is a description of the operation of the magnetron magnet.
The magnet is around the magnetron. The magnet gives the strong magnetic field that changes
the RF power output of the magnetron into the transverse electric field. This field is necessary to
move through the rectangular section of the waveguide system.

The TCS controls the DC power for the magnet through the LV PSU.
A water circuit keeps the magnetron magnet cool.
4.6.8.5 Operation of the launch section
This is a description of the operation of the launch section which is part of the RF system.
The magnetron magnet operates with the launch section to supply the RF power to the
rectangular waveguide.
4.6.8.6 Operation of the RF power output
This is a description of the operation of the RF power output.
In the low energy modes, the magnetron delivers a peak power of approximately 2.5 MW. In the
high energy modes, it gives a peak power of approximately 5 MW.
To get these levels, the peak power is automatically adjusted through the energy range of the
machine. This is from a nominal 2.5 MW at 4 MeV to 5 MW at 25 MeV. There are average power
limits to the magnetron, therefore the PRF rate in the high energy mode is half of that in the low
energy mode. The correct conditions for operation for the magnetron are set automatically when
the necessary mode is selected.
4.6.8.7 Operation of the effects of temperature on the magnetron
This is a description of the operation of the effects of temperature on the magnetron.
The operation of the magnetron causes an increase in temperature which can change the
dimensions of the device. These changes can have an effect on the frequency of operation. See
Figure 4.13 for the magnetron temperature curve.

with a hot filament, and an anode which is a focus electrode. The body of the electron gun body
has two primary parts that are sealed together:

4.6.9.1 Operation of the electron emission of the electron gun
This is a description of the operation of the electron emissions of the electron gun.
The cathode of the electron gun is energized by the same pulses that are applied to the cathode
of the magnetron. R6 and the capacitance of the HT cable and the gun transformer give a delay to
the pulses. When the negative voltage is applied (at a maximum of -50 kV), a pulse of electrons is
output from the cathode to make a space charge. This increases the speed of the electrons, at a
velocity of approximately 0.4 C, in the direction of the first cell of the injector section of the
waveguide.
The shape of the gun, and that of the input mode transformer, changes the field gradient to move
the electrons along the axis of the waveguide. The electrons are then and put into the injector
section.

4.6.9.2 Operation of the electron gun initial acceleration
This is a description of the operation of how the electron gun gives the initial acceleration in the
speed of the electrons.
To increase the speed of the electrons through the waveguide the axial RF electric field is
maximized. The phase velocity of the RF wave is synchronized to the velocity of the electrons. The
high voltage pulse at the cathode initially increases the speed of the electrons to 0.4 C. The RF is
synchronized with the RF, which decreases to 0.4 C by the first cell of the injector section of the
waveguide.
4.6.9.3 Operation of the gun filament control
This is a description of the operation of the gun filament control.
The electron gun is part of a servo system that keeps a constant beam energy in the X-ray mode
and a constant output dose rate in the electron mode. This is controlled by the gun filament
current, and therefore the beam current.
The electron energy from the linear accelerator is in proportion to the beam current. In the X-ray
mode, this property controls the beam energy. Signals from the inner and outer hump beam
intensity detectors of the ionization chamber in the BLD sense the changes in energy. The signals
that result adjust the beam current by control of the gun filament current to keep a constant
beam energy.
In the electron mode the beam current is small. Therefore electron energy operates almost
independently of the level of the beam current. But, small changes in beam current change the
dose rate. The dose rate signal from the ion chamber is therefore controls the gun filament
current to keep a constant dose rate.
4.6.9.4 Operation of the gun filament power supply
This is a description of the operation of the gun filament power supply.
The PPG PCB and two UPD PCBs supply the controlled AC electrical power for the heater current
at the gun filament as follows:
• 400 Hz for a 50 Hz primary electrical supply
• 360 Hz for a 60 Hz primary electrical supply.
The PPG PCB and the UPD PCBs increase the amplitude of the sine wave signal. This increases the
temperature of the gun filament. The sine wave that the PPG PCB makes, is synchronized with the
PRF. Therefore the gun filament current is always at zero when the modulator starts. This makes
sure that the electrons stay in focus.
4.6.9.5 Operation of the beam current
This is a description of the operation of the beam current of the electron gun.
The RF power and the gun filament current control the size of the beam current at an applicable
energy, (see Figure 4.15.
