Product description of the HT and RF systems
System description of the RF system
Item Description
Radiation absorber A small proportion of high energy RF leaks through glass
envelope of the magnetron. The radiation absorber makes sure
that the energy leak is absorbed (by conversion to heat), near
the magnetron.
RF isolator The RF isolator inhibits magnetron moding effects by preventing
the accumulation of standing waves in the transition section of
the waveguide. The isolator passes the forward RF but absorbs
the reflected waves. In the forward direction, RF is passed with
losses of only 0.6 dB (approximately): in the reverse direction
there is at least a 13 dB rejection of RF. The isolator is therefore
an almost constant impedance to the magnetron, so reflected
waves are rejected.
The isolator has a length of rectangular waveguide to which a
large, fixed external magnet. In the isolator there is a set of
internal ferrite plates that are magnetically connected to the
magnet. In the magnetic field, the magnetic elements of the
ferrite align to give a low loss to forward RF and a high loss to
reflected waves.
The RF isolator is water-cooled. The internal ferrite plates are
covered by ceramic Berylia plates. These conduct the heat in
the ferrite to the walls of the isolator. The heat is then
decreased by the cooling water system.
Directional sidewall coupler The directional sidewall coupler is part of the residual RF
feedback path. Its purpose is to transfer an optimum amount of
the returning RF from the accelerating waveguide to the RF
output of the magnetron.
The directional sidewall coupler has two connected sections of
rectangular waveguide, with a number of holes in the adjacent
walls. The holes are set at a distance of ¼ wavelength apart.
When in phase, the RF goes into the section that moves the
source RF from the magnetron. This is approximately 4 dB,
therefore approximately half of the RF power is sent back. The
balance is sent to the RF load, where it is removed as heat (see
Figure 4.17).
RF load The RF load is a section of waveguide that tapers at one end.
The change in impedance caused by the different physical
dimensions of the taper in the waveguide changes the RF to
heat. This is subsequently removed by the cooling water
system.
The RF load has an above temperature switch that operates
with the interlock system.
RF input window and flexible The rectangular waveguide is pressurized to approximately
waveguide 0.8 bar by a dielectric gas that prevents high tension breakdown
in the waveguide. The accelerating waveguide is evacuated to
better than 10-5 m bar. Two ceramic windows separate these
extremes of pressure, one at the input to the accelerating
waveguide and one at the output.

4.6.11.2 Operation of the accelerating waveguide (linear accelerator)
This is a description of the operation of the accelerating waveguide.
The accelerating waveguide has two cylinder shape waveguide sections that are connected at the
center. One section of the accelerating waveguide is known as the injector (or buncher) section
and the other is known as the relativistic section.
Each section has a cylinder shape copper waveguide. These have copper irises along their length
to make a number of dome-shaped resonant cavities. The two sections connect at the center
joint to make one accelerating waveguide. The center joint has a RF probe that the automatic
frequency control (AFC) system uses.
The injector section has different spaces between the irises. The first six cavities of this section
accelerate the RF field and, therefore, the electrons. In the relativistic section, there is a small
increase in the velocity of the wave, and gain in electron energy is because of the related increase
in mass.
A vacuum is kept in the accelerating waveguide by two, 35 liters/second ion pumps - one at each
end of the waveguide. The vacuum is sealed at the two mode transformers, and at the center
joint, by indium wire which helps electrical conduction.
Small changes in temperature can change the dimensions of the accelerating waveguide. This can
have an effect on the tuning and performance of the linear accelerator. The accelerating
waveguide assembly has a water-cooled casing, the temperature of which is very accurately
controlled.
4.6.11.3 Operation of the input and output mode transformers
This is a description of the operation of the input and output mode transformers of the
accelerating waveguide.
There is a mode transformer at each end of the accelerating waveguide assembly. The mode
transformers are installed in the first (input) and last (output) resonant cavities of the accelerating
waveguide.
Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator Document ID: 45133702097_13
Corrective Maintenance Manual HT and RF Systems
© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 75

Product description of the HT and RF systems
System description of the RF system
The input mode transformer changes the RF from the TE mode in the rectangular waveguide into
the TM mode that is necessary to accelerate the electrons. The round section of the input mode
transformer is the first cavity of the accelerating waveguide. The RF is input to the transformer
from the side.
The input mode transformer turns the RF through 90°. The RF electric field, which is at a
maximum between the long walls of the rectangular waveguide, aligns with the RF electric field
which is axial in the accelerating waveguide. The slope of the cell in the waveguide gives the
phase velocity of the RF, which aligns that of the linear accelerator.
The size of the rectangular section of the input mode transformer is decreased in two steps. This
decrease in the impedance that results causes heat. The input mode transformer is therefore
water-cooled.
The output mode transformer removes the RF from the accelerating waveguide and changes the
electrical Field Mode from TM to TE to move through the feedback section of the rectangular
waveguide.
4.6.11.4 Operation of the RF output window and flexible waveguide
This is a description of the operation of the RF output window and the flexible waveguide.
The RF output window keeps the evacuated accelerating waveguide from the gas pressurized
feedback section of the rectangular waveguide.
The section of waveguide before the RF output window is flexible to give the connection of the
90° twisted section of waveguide.
4.6.11.5 Operation of the 90° twisted waveguide section
This is a description of the operation of the twisted section of the waveguide.
After the output flexible waveguide section, the RF goes through a waveguide section that is
twisted through 90°. The twisted section adjusts the phase of the RF to align with the sidewall
coupler.
4.6.11.6 Operation of the high power phase shifter
This is a description of the operation of the high power phase shifter.
The high power (HP) phase shifter adjusts the phase angle of the RF to align it with the phase
angle of the input RF. This correction increases the primary RF from the magnetron, which is
necessary to get the highest energies.
The HP phase shifter has two sections of rectangular waveguide connected together to make a
3 dB directional coupler. Each of the waveguide sections has a coplanar short circuit piston. The
motors operate the two pistons to move in and out of the related waveguide section together.

This is a description of the operation of the RF load.
The RF load absorbs the remaining RF that is not put back through the sidewall coupler. This

4.6.13.1 Operation of the interface PCB
This is a description of the operation of the interface PCB used in the FTM rack.
The interface PCB supplies the drive signals to the UPD PCBs. The PCB also monitors the fans and
DC power rails of the FTM rack.
The interface PCB receives demand and enable signals from the HTCA and changes them into
drive signals for the UPD PCBs in the FTM rack. The PCB also processes the voltage and the
current samples of the UPD PCB outputs to give feedback data to the TCS.
The interface PCB also receives a demand from the AO12-HTA PCB and changes it into a drive
signal to the PSU. The interface PCB then processes the voltage and current samples from the PSU
outputs to give feedback data to the TCS.

4.6.14 Operation of the automatic frequency control
This is a description of the operation of the automatic frequency control system.
The automatic frequency control (AFC) system has:
• The PPG PCBs
• UPD PCBs
• The LP phase shifter
• The phase error detector
• The magnetron tuner.
See (Figure 4.22) for information about operation of the AFC system.

The linear accelerator operates on a different RF frequency for each of the energy modes. In
some modes, the frequency of operation is such that the electrons and the moving RF wave are
synchronized. In other modes, to give a lower beam energy, the frequency of operation lets the

System description of the RF system
electrons move in phase in relation to the moving RF wave. Also, temperature effects in the
waveguide and the magnetron result in changes to the wavelength.
The resonant frequency of the magnetron must be controlled continuously. This is to make sure
that the phase difference in the moving RF wave between the input and the output of the
accelerating waveguide stays constant. To do this the AFC system, which directly controls the
magnetron tuner and therefore makes sure that the magnetron operates at the correct frequency
while the HT is on.
The system gets a sample of the phase difference in the moving RF wave at two different points. It
then uses the error value that results to adjust the position of the magnetron anode to cancel the
phase error. The samples are from the RF probe at the gun end of the waveguide: and from the
RF probe at the center joint.
4.6.14.1 Operation of the automatic positioning of the magnetron tuner in the capture
range
This is a description of the operation of the automatic positioning of the magnetron tuner in the
capture range.
The capture range of the AFC system makes it is necessary to set the magnetron tuner to the
correct position in the capture range when the HT is switched on. This must apply at during a cold
start or multiple treatments. The tuner rest item Item 97 from the TCS, controls the automatic
position of the magnetron tuner in the capture range.
The AFC servo controls the position of the magnetron tuner while the magnetron and the linear
accelerator warm up. First, the TCS sets the tuner rest position. When an energy is selected and
the HT is switched on, the LP phase shifter (which is already in position), moves the phase error
detector to set the RF phase error to a zero. This is the rest position. The LP phase shifter has no
more functions while the linear accelerator delivers the selected energy. This becomes active
again only when a new energy is selected and the HT is switched on again.
When the HT is switched on, the magnetron tuner starts at its current position (the rest position if
the HT has been off for some time). In Auto mode, after each pulse, the PPG PCB calculates the
phase error, which corrects the position of the tuner. When the HT is switched off, the TCS
continues control until the tuner goes back to the rest position.
When the HT is switched off, the TCS senses the temperature decrease of the linear accelerator
and records the last position of the magnetron tuner. The TCS then moves the magnetron tuner
gradually. Therefore if the HT is switched on again, the magnetron tuner position (frequency) is in
the capture range of the AFC system.
The magnetron can be tuned down only if power supplies are available. If power supplies are not
available, then the position is calculated and the magnetron tuner is moved to the correct
position when power is restored.
4.6.14.2 Operation of the phase adjustment
This is a description of the operation of the phase adjustment.
The two samples of the moving RF wave are applied to the phase error detector. At the rest
position, the phase error detector continues to monitor the RF phase difference at the two
sample points. This gives two different phase leg outputs to the PPG PCB. There is one output for
each phase signal, with a phase difference of 90° between them (imposed by the phase error
detector). On the PPG PCB, the phase leg signals are calculated and sent to the TCS. The phase leg
signals are also compared in the PPG PCB to give an error signal to the TCS.
The current through the solenoid shows the actual position of the solenoid magnet. The current
is monitored, and this data is sent back to the TCS as part of the position servo control loop.

4.6.14.3 Operation of the LP phase shifter
This is a description of the operation of the LP phase shifter.
The LP phase shifter is installed in Area 45 on the gantry drum (see ).
The assembly has a motor energized by UMD PCB 11-5 in the motor drive PCBs rack (Area 11).
The motor drive is connected to the phase adjuster in the phase error detector through a gearbox
with the drive ratio 30:1.
The LP phase shifter sets the RF phase error to a zero at the applicable frequency of operation for
the selected energy (f ) when the HT is switched on. Item Item 555 from the TCS controls the
o
movement of the LP phase shifter. At f , the motor moves the phase adjuster in the phase error
o
detector by the quantity as given by Item 555. This adjusts the path length of the RF sample
signal from the gun end until it is in phase with the signal from the center joint.
The limit switches (SW1 and SW2) monitor the movement of the motor. Two diodes (D1 and D2)
let the motor move in the opposite direction if more than the limit.
A potentiometer RV1 monitors the position of the motor and read by the TCS as monitor item
Item 230.
4.6.14.4 Operation of the phase error detector
This is a description of the operation of the phase error detector.
The phase error detector assembly is installed in Area 45 on the gantry drum (see Figure 4.23).

• RF diode rectifiers
The phase error detector is symmetrical and has two input ports and two output ports.
The samples from the RF probes at the gun end and center joint of the waveguide system are
applied to different inputs of the hybrid coupler. The phase adjuster is connected in the line from
the probe at the gun end.

Product description of the HT and RF systems
System description of the HT and RF system control
The phase error detector decreases the RF power, and one signal moves in phase by 90° from the
other. The output ports therefore give an in phase part and a 90° phase moved part of the input
signals.
When the signal amplitudes at the inputs are equal and in phase, the amplitudes at outputs are
equal and the phase error is at zero. When a difference between the input signals occurs, a
difference that can be measured in amplitude, is found at the outputs.
The outputs of the phase error detector are applied to the TCS through the HTCA.

The switch devices of the HT PSU supply 600 V DC to the charge transformer. The charge

The thyratron discharges the PFN with the THY TRIG control signal supplied by the modulator.

4.7.2.3 Operation of the HT pulse generation
This is a description of the operation of the HT pulse generation.
With Item 433, Item 434 and Item 443 set, the FPGA on the PPG PCB sets internal signal HCE to
the PPG PCB. The circuit sets signal HT_CHARGE_EN to logic 0 for 25 µs at the PRF. This is then
input to the HTC PCB as signal CHARGE ENABLE.
The HTC PCB, changes the CHARGE ENABLE input to signal HT DRIVE PULSE. The HTC PCB stops
the HT DRIVE PULSE if the protection circuits sense one or more of the fault conditions:
• Multiple charges by noise
• HT overload
• The voltage on the primary side of the charging transformer is too high
• Reverse diode overload.
If no fault condition found, the HT DRIVE PULSE is sent through a coaxial cable to the HTCA optical
receiver. The FTCA Optical receiver isolates the signal. The signal is then routed through the HT
isolation unit to HT PSU PCBs.
The output of the HT PSU PCBs switch on the devices to set the 600 V DC supply to the charging
transformer.
The HT DRIVE PULSE controls the FET switches on the HT PSU PCBs, which quickly turn the
switches off at the end of the charge pulse period. This removes the 600 V supply from the
primary side of the charging transformer.

4.7.2.4 Operation of the thyratron pulse generation
This is a description of the operation of the thyratron pulse generation.
When Item 433, Item 434 and Item 443 are set, the FPGA on the PPG PCB the internal signal
THY_TRIG is input to the thyratron signal circuit. The output of this circuit is the THY_TRIG signal.
This is then supplied to the modulator through the HTCA optical receiver and the modulator
isolation PCB.
In the modulator, the THY_TRIG signal is supplied to the thyratron trigger PCB in the thyratron
pulser. The thyratron trigger PCB conditions the trigger signal. Therefore when THY_TRIG is set, a
positive pulse of approximately 900 V of a time of three µs is supplied to grid G2 of the thyratron.
This causes the thyratron to discharge the PFN through the primary side of the pulse transformer.
To keep the fast operation of the thyratron, the full 900 V pulse appears at grid G2 only when the
device is cold. When the thyratron is warm, only the edge part of the pulse is used (see Figure
4.25).

4.7.3.3 Operation of the HT overload signals
This is a description of the operation of the HT overload signals of the protection circuits.
The protection circuits monitor an HT overload condition at the secondary side of the charging
transformer. This is supplied to the HT crowbar detector PCB. In a fault condition, the voltage of
the charging transformer increases to 25 kV and therefore applies 1500 V (6% of 25 kV) to the HT
crowbar detector PCB. A diode circuit on the HT crowbar detector PCB operates to give an output
HT OVERLOAD DETECTOR. This signal is supplied to the HTC PCB through the HT isolation PCB and
the HTCA optical receiver.
The input at the HTC PCB as signal HT O/L DET I/P, prevents the HT DRIVE PULSE output from the
HTC PCB if there is an HT overload.
The input to the HTC PCB is supplied to the DIE-HTB PCB as signal HT O/L DET O/P. The DIE-HTB
PCB then supplies the monitor item Item 83 for the TCS. If there is an HT overload, the FPGA on
the DIE-HTB PCB sets Item 83 to the value 0. Usually Item 83 is kept at the value 1.
4.7.3.4 Operation of the reverse diode overload signals
This is a description of the operation of the reverse diode overload signals of the protection
circuits.
The reverse diode overload detector in the modulator isolation unit senses unwanted reverse
current flow in the modulator. The modulator isolation PCB develops a monitor voltage from the
HT CHARGE signal. The modulator isolation unit measures the level of this voltage. This gives an
active high signal REV DIODE O/L. This signal goes through the HTCA optical receiver and is an
input to the HTC PCB as signal REV DIODE O/L I/P.
On the HTC PCB, the active REV DIODE O/L I/P signal prevents the HT DRIVE PULSE output from
the HTC PCB if there is a reverse diode overload.
The signal REV DIODE O/L O/P is input to the DIE-HTB PCB. The DIE-HTB PCB supplies monitor
item Item 84 for the TCS. If there is a reverse current flow in the modulator, the FPGA on the DIE-
HTB PCB sets Item 84 to the value 0. Usually Item 84 is held at the value 1.
4.7.3.5 Operation of the modulator temperature signals
This is a description of the operation of the modulator temperature signals of the protection
circuits.
A sensor in the pulse transformer monitors the temperature of the modulator. The output of the
temperature sensor is an input to the modulator isolation unit PCB as signal MODULATOR O/T.
This signal is an output from the modulator isolation unit and input to the DIE-HTB PCB through
the HTCA optical receiver.
The DIE-HTB PCB supplies monitor item Item 87 for the TCS. If the modulator temperature is too
high, the FPGA on the DIE-HTB PCB sets Item 87 to the value 0. Usually Item 87 is held at the
value 1.
4.7.3.6 Operation of the PFN voltage monitor signal
This is a description of the operation of the pulse forming network (PFN) signals of the protection
circuits.
The PFN voltage monitor circuit gives the voltage protection at the PFN. Level detectors in the
modulator isolation unit set different voltage thresholds for the high and low energy modes. This
gives signals PFN V MON H.E. and PFN V MON L.E.

Product description of the HT and RF systems
System description of the HT and RF system control
• The signal PFN V MON H.E, shows if the voltage threshold is too high in high energy
mode.
• Signal PFN V MON L.E, shows if the voltage threshold is too high in low energy mode.
These signals are supplied to the HTC PCB through the HTCA optical receiver.
On the HTC PCB, the active PFN voltage monitor signal (PFN V MON H.E. or PFN V MON L.E.) stops
the HT DRIVE PULSE output from the HTC PCB if the voltage threshold is too high.
Control item Item 420 from the TCS shows the selected energy mode. This is supplied to the ROC-
HTA PCB. When this signal is set to logic 0, the low power mode is selected: when the signal is set
to logic 1, the high power mode is selected. The signal is applied to the HTC PCB, as the H/L
ENERGY STROBE.
The signals are put together with the H/L ENERGY STROBE to supply a monitor signal to the DIE-
HTB PCB. The monitor item Item 88 is read by the TCS. Item, Item 88 shows the status of the
charge of the HT system. If the PFN voltage threshold is too high in one of the two modes, the
FPGA on the DIE-HTB PCB sets Item 88 to the value 0. Usually Item 88 is held at the value 1.
4.7.3.7 Operation of the HT crowbar detection signals
This is a description of the operation of the HT crowbar detection signals.
The HT crowbar detector PCB senses if the PFN voltage is about to go above the rate of charge.
This condition is usually caused by unwanted operation of the switches by noise. The output of
the HT crowbar detector PCB is signal CROWBAR DETECTOR. This is then input to the HTC PCB
through the HT isolation PCB and the HTCA optical receiver. The input to the HTC PCB is
CROWBAR DET I/P.
On the HTC PCB, the CROWBAR DET I/P signal prevents the HT DRIVE PULSE output from the HTC
PCB if a crowbar operates.
The signal CROWBAR O/P is applied to the DIE-HTB PCB. The DIE-HTB PCB supplies monitor item
Item 89 to be read by the TCS. If the PFN charge voltage is too high, the FPGA on the DIE-HTB PCB
sets Item 89 to the value 0. Usually Item 89 is held at the value 1.
4.7.3.8 Operation of the HT PSU temperature signals
This is a description of the operation of the HT PSU temperature signals.
The DIE-HTB PCB monitors the HT OVERTEMP DETECTOR signal that shows the temperature
status of the HT PSU. The signal source is at the bellows switch (SW2) of the charging transformer
and the heat sink fan (SW1). The signal is applied to the DIE-HTB PCB through the HT isolation
PCB and HTCA optical receiver.
The DIE-HTB PCB supplies monitor item Item 251 for the TCS. If the measured temperature is to
high, the FPGA on the DIE-HTB PCB sets Item 251 to the value 0. The value of Item 251 is 1 during
usual conditions of operation.
4.7.3.9 Operation of the charging transformer current control signals
This is a description of the operation of the charging transformer current control signals.
The rate of charge is set by the control item Item 330 from the TCS. This is the maximum current
permitted at the primary side of the charging transformer. The control item is an input to the
AO12-HTA PCB as a 12-bit analog word. This then used by the AO12-HTA PCB to set the voltage of
the HT threshold. This value is then input to the HTC PCB as signal PRI I REF I/P.
A current transformer on the HT PSU heat sink assembly senses the current level at the primary
side of the charging transformer. Signal PRI I MON shows the monitored current. This is an input
to the HTC PCB as signal PRI I I/P.

Product description of the HT and RF systems
System description of the HT and RF system control
In the X-ray mode, signals from the beam intensity detectors of the ion chamber in the BLD sense
changes in energy. These then start adjustment of the gun filament current.
The Electron mode uses the dose rate signal to set the gun filament current.
Together these parameters, and other TCS control items, set the applicable level of gun current
set item.
The TCS sets the level of the gun filament current through a demand signal, from set item of the
gun current. The demand signal starts in the PPG PCB and goes through the MT GF drive interface
PCB to the gun filament UPD PCBs. The UPD PCBs supply the current pulses at the level that is
necessary to keep a constant beam energy.
The gun filament current pulse occurs in the middle of the null part of the waveform of the
applied voltage by applicable adjustment of the thyratron trigger pulse from the PPG PCB. This
stops the flows of the current in the filament during that part of the waveform. This stops
magnetic field effects on the beam from the gun filament.
If the conditions for supply of the gun filament are not correct, an enable signal stops the gun
filament current.

4.7.4.3 Operation of the gun filament current control parameters
This a description of the operation of the gun filament control parameters.
The machine state of the linear accelerator has an effect on the control current of the electron
gun filament as follows:
• Standby - The electron gun is on, but the gun filament current is too low to make
radiation.
• Manual - The electron gun is on and the gun filament current level set by a value input
at the TCS. This setting is not available from Clinical Mode.
• Auto - In Auto, the electron gun is on and the gun filament current is servo controlled
about a level given by the calibration block.
The Item 327 is the gun control servo block, which sets the gun filament current in X-ray mode.
Item Item 538 transmits the command to the AO12-HTA PCB. Item Item 201 enables the
applicable circuits from the X-ray/Electron and Auto/Manual mode settings (see Table 4.6).

The value of Item 381 is the gun filament current level during servo operation in Auto mode. In X-
ray mode, Item Item 186 controls the loop gain of the gun servo. In Electron mode, the value of
Item 381 is controlled by Item 187, which shows the necessary dose rate. Items Item 186 and
Item 187 are 12-bit analog words.
The Item 181 sets the gun servo control system between the Auto and Manual modes.
The Item 161 is the magnetron tuner control block. This shows the magnetron frequency as the
number of turns by the magnetron tuner. If the frequency is incorrect, Item 161 sets the gun to
the standby condition and the filament current is then set by the value of Item 380.
The Item 416 selects the gun filament current as the output channel of the AO12-HTA PCB to be
monitored as Item 393 and read by the TCS.
The Item 217 is the monitored electron gun current obtained from the gun filament UPD PCBs via
the MT/GF drive interface PCB. This provides the feedback element of the servo loop.
4.7.4.4 Operation of the gun filament current demand signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the gun filament current demand signal.
The gun filament current value of Item 538 is an input to the AO12-HTA PCB, which changes the
12-bit digital input into an analog signal. This signal is an input to the PPG PCB.
The PPG PCB has a field programmable gate array (FPGA). This changes the gun filament current
to the GUN_FIL_DEMAND signal for the UPD PCBs. The GUN_CONTROL signal from the SIB
controls the amplitude of the GUN_FIL_DEMAND signal to set the applicable X-ray or Electron
mode. The GUN_FIL_DEMAND signal is also synchronized to the thyratron start signal. Therefore
when the radiation pulse is generated, the gun filament current is zero.
The GUN FIL DEMAND signal from the PPG PCB gives an input to the MT/GF interface PCB. The
MT/GF changes the current value of the demand to a voltage. This is sent to UPD A of the bridge
gun filament UPD PCBs through the backplane of the FTM rack.
The gun filament UPD PCBs use the voltage of the GUN FIL DEMAND signal to supply the correct
AC current pulses to the filament of the electron gun. UPD B supplies the positive pulse and UPD
A the negative pulse.
4.7.4.5 Operation of the gun filament current enable signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the gun filament signal processing.
The PPG PCB also supplies a gun enable signal GUN_FIL_EN. This signal is permanently enabled
(logic 0).
The GUN_FIL_EN output from the PPG PCB is input to the ROC-HTA PCB. The signal goes through
the contacts of a relay (RLB2). Relay RLB2 puts vacuum system interlock conditions into the gun
filament control circuit. The fault conditions are shown by monitor items that are output by the
vacuum system through the DIE-HTB PCB:
• Item 227 - the vacuum pressure at the gun end of the waveguide.
• Item 238 - the 10-4 vacuum trip signal for the gun end of the waveguide system.
• Item 493 - shows a fault in the vacuum readout system.
If the vacuum pressure is incorrect or there is a fault in the vacuum monitor system, the contacts
of RLB2 open . This disconnects the GUN FILAMENT ENABLE signal from the MT/GF interface PCB.
The MT/GF interface PCB monitors some power supply rails and the cooling fans of the FTM rack.
If there is a fault, it removes the GUN FILAMENT ENABLE signal to remove the gun filament
current. The GUN FILAMENT ENABLE signal is set to logic 0 during usual conditions.
From the MT/GF interface PCB, the GUN FILAMENT ENABLE signal is applied to the gun filament
UPD PCBs. If there is a fault, the signal is set to a logic 1. This stops the outputs of the UPD PCBs
to switch off the gun filament current.

4.7.4.6 Operation of the gun filament current and voltage monitor signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the gun filament current and voltage monitor signal
processing.
The current and voltage levels of the output of the gun filament UPD PCBs are monitored by the
TCS. On each PCB, a sample of the current output and a voltage derived from the current output
are applied to the MT/GF interface PCB. This is as signals GUN FIL I and GUN FIL V.
The MT/GF interface PCB mixes the two current samples to give a one signal GUN FIL I. Signal
GUN FIL I is input to the SCC-HTA and AI12-HTA PCBs, the output of which is a 12-bit digital value.
This signal is read by the TCS as monitor item Item 217.
The two voltage samples are also mixed to give a single signal GUN FIL V. This is a voltage that is
the RMS current supplied by the gun filament UPD PCBs. Signal GUN FIL V is input to the SCC-HTA
and AI12-HTA PCBs, the output of which is a 12-bit digital value that is read by the TCS as monitor
item Item 216.
4.7.4.7 Operation of the gun filament demand monitor signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the gun filament demand monitor signal processing.
A sample of the analog version of the gun filament signal is read by the TCS. Item Item 416 selects
the gun filament signal as the output channel of the monitor multiplexer on the AO12-HTA PCB.
This signal is input to the SCC-HTA and AI12-HTA PCBs, the output of which is a 12-bit digital value
that is read by the TCS as monitor item Item 393.
4.7.5 Operation of the magnetron filament control
This is a description of the operation of the control circuit of the magnetron filament.
The filament of the magnetron cathode makes the cathode hot to control the emission of
electrons. When the magnetron operates, electrons that are sent back increase the temperature
of the cathode increases by the electrons that are sent back. The effect is in proportion to the HT
current and therefore, to keep the cathode at the correct temperature, the filament current is
decreased.
A programmed PSU supplies the magnetron filament in the gun filament PSU (Area 33). The TCS
controls the PSU through the AO12-HTA PCB. The PPG PCB gives synchronization pulses to make
sure that the filament supply is blank before each occurrence of the magnetron trigger.
See Figure 4.28 for a picture of the magnetron filament signal paths.

4.7.5.3 Operation of the magnetron filament control signal processing
This is a description of the operation of how the magnetron filament uses the control signals.
The Item 326 controls the filament voltage. It compares the monitored values of Item 212 and
Item 244 against a table of the HT current magnetron filament voltage. The calibration block
keeps this value Item 537 as adjusts this value as is necessary.
The value of the magnetron filament current,Item 537 is input to the AO12-HTA PCB. This
changes the 12-bit digital input into an analog signal. This is then input to the MT/GF interface as
signal CONTROL. The current level of the signal is changed to a voltage level and input to the
programmable PSU as the IPROG.
The programmable PSU supplies electrical power to the magnetron filament. The current level of
the output is set by the value of the IPROG input.
4.7.5.4 Operation of the magnetron rundown signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the how the magnetron filament uses the rundown
signals.
The current transformer T4 on the HT PSU heat sink assembly monitors the current in the primary
coil of the charging transformer. Signal PRI I MON shows the current. This is input to the HTC PCB
through the HT isolation unit and the HTCA optical receiver.
On the HTC PCB the PRI I MON signal is compared against a preset value. The result is input to the
SCC-HTB and AI12-HTB PCBs as output PRI MON.
The AI12-HTA PCB then changes the analog value of the PRI MON signal into 12-bit digital data for
Item 244. The TCS then supplies this to the magnetron filament rundown law to Item 326.
4.7.5.5 Operation of the magnetron filament current and voltage monitoring
This is a description of the operation of how the HT and RF control system monitors the
magnetron filament current and voltage.

On the AI12-HTA PCB, the analog values of the input signals are changed into 12-bit digital data.
The signals then become monitor items Item 212 and Item 213, which are read by the TCS.
4.7.6 Operation of the magnetron magnet control
This is a description of the magnetron control signal processing.
The magnetron magnet control circuit consists of:
• Magnetron control signals
• Magnetron monitor signals
• Magnetron demand signals.
See for a description of the magnetron magnet control circuits.
Item Description
Magnetron control signals The LV PSU (Area 13) supplies the DC power for the solenoid
magnet of the magnetron. Software item Item 328 holds the
level of the power and sends a value to Item 539, the
magnetron magnet current set item. Item Item 539 is input to
the AO12-RHA PCB, which changes the 12-bit digital input into
analog output signal CONTROL.
The CONTROL output of the AO12-RHA PCB is applied to LV PSU
control PCB 13D in the LV PSU. The PCB supplies a trigger signal
to the full-wave bridge rectifier and LC network in the LV PSU.
This supplies the current to the coil of the magnetron magnet.
Magnetron monitor signals The magnetron magnet current is monitored as signal I
MONITOR by a resistor in the LC network. This is sent back to
the LV PSU control PCB 13D. The current value is input to the
SCC-RHA and AI12-RHA PCBs, which supplies Item 328 for the
TCS.
The magnetron magnet voltage is monitored on signal V
MONITOR. This is sent back to the SCC-RHB and AI12-RHB PCBs
from the positive side of the LC network. This supplies item
Item 214 to the TCS.
Magnetron demand signals The analog version of the magnetron magnet demand signal is
read by the TCS. The Item 416 selects the CONTROL signal as
the output channel of the monitor multiplexer on the AO12-
RHA PCB. This version of the CONTROL signal is an input to the
SCC-RHA and AI12-RHA PCBs. The output from these PCBs is a
12-bit digital value that is read by the TCS as monitor item Item
394.

4.7.7 Operation of the magnetron tuner and AFC control circuits
This is a description of the operation of the magnetron tuner and the AFC control circuits.
When the HT is off, the magnetron tuner is always at the rest position shown by control item Item
97. This position is set during the test program and is correct for operation at the lowest PRF rate
of six pulses for each second.
The TCS quickly adjusts the tuner to keep the correct frequency during the initial warm up period.
After the warm up, a RF beam is made and RF phase angle information is supplied to the phase
error detector in the AFC system.
The phase angle signals make a mixed phase error signal that is sent from the AFC system to the
magnetron tuner servo. The error signal can be positive or negative, and causes the tuner
solenoid to move the tuning prongs in the direction necessary to keep the resonant frequency of
the magnetron.

4.7.7.4 Operation of the magnetron tuner demand signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the magnetron tuner demand signal processing.
To move the magnetron tuner to the correct start position:
• Item Item 161 uses the position information from Item 231
• Item Item 161 uses the cold start value from Item 97 or the warm start value from the
calibration block.
The warm start value changes with the time since the HT is switched off. The correct point on the
cool curve is continuously calculated and is used when the HTCA power supplies are on.
In the Auto mode, Item 182 is set to 1 and the phase error, shown by Item 236, tunes the
magnetron for the optimization output, if:
1 The PPG PCB transmits the sample pulses when Item 444 is set to 1.
2 The value of Item 232 and Item 233 is greater than 5.
The 12-bit digital value of Item 553 is input to the FPGA on the PPG PCB. The data word
calculated by the FPGA changes to an analog value. This is then applied to the MT/GF drive
interface PCB as signal MAG TUNE DEMAND.
The MT/GF drive interface PCB, changes the current value to a voltage. This is sent to UPD A of
the magnetron tuner UPD PCBs through the same mode noise suppression filter.
Together the magnetron tuner UPD PCBs use the voltage of the MAG TUNE DEMAND signal to
give the correct AC current pulses to the solenoid of the magnetron tuner. UPD B supplies the
positive pulse and UPD A the negative pulse. The solenoid is energized and the tuning is moved to
the position in the spaces of the magnetron anode.
4.7.7.5 Operation of the magnetron tuner enable signal processing
The magnetron tuner enable signal MAG_TUNE_EN is supplied by the PPG PCB. This signal is
permanently enabled (logic 0).
The MAG_TUNE_EN output from the PPG PCB is an input to the MT/GF drive interface PCB. This
PCBs monitors some power supply rails and the fans of the FTM rack . If it senses a fault, it sets
the MAG TUNER ENABLE to logic 1 to remove power from the magnetron tuner. If not, the MAG
TUNER ENABLE signal stays at (logic 0).
From the MT/GF drive interface PCB, the MAG TUNER ENABLE signal is supplied at the same time
to the two magnetron tuner UPD PCBs. If a fault sets the signal to (logic 1), the output drivers of
the UPD PCBs are prevented. This sets the magnetron tuner current off.

4.7.7.6 Operation of the LP phase shifter drive signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the LP phase shifter
To set the phase error detector to the correct position, the calibration block supplies Item 163
with information about the temperature and the motor position of the LP phase shifter. This sets
the value of Item 555.
The 8-bit digital value of Item 555 is an input to the AO8-HTA PCB. This supplies the signal SET
SPEED to UMD PCB 11-5. The amplitude and polarity of the SET SPEED signal (±5 V) show the
necessary speed and direction of rotation of the motor of the LP phase shifter.
UMD PCB 11-5 is permanently enabled by the logic 0 (CGPS) connection. The PCB has a SET
SPEED signal which causes a pulse wave modulated signal for the motor of the LP phase shifter.
The motor then rotates and operates the phase adjuster in the phase error detector to the
position, at which the RF phase error is at zero.
4.7.7.7 Operation of the LP phase shifter monitor signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the LP phase shifter monitor signal processing.
The potentiometer RV1 monitors the movement of the LP phase shifter motor against +10V
supply from the SCC-HTB PCB. The potentiometer supplies a POSITION signal to Channel 11 of the
AI12-HTB PCB. This changes the position voltage to a 12-bit digital value for the TCS as monitor
item Item 230.
A sample of the analog version of the LP phase shifter signal is read by the TCS. Item Item 409
selects the SET SPEED signal as the output channel of the monitor multiplexer on the AO8-HTA
PCB. This version of the SET SPEED signal is input to the SCC-HTB and AI12-HTB PCBs. This gives
an 8-bit digital value that is read by the TCS as monitor item Item 391.
4.7.7.8 Operation of the phase error signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the phase error signal processing.
The signal GUN RF3 from the RF probe at the input window of the rectangular waveguide is
supplied to input A of the hybrid coupler. This is through the phase adjuster. Signal CJ RF4 from
the RF probe at the center joint of the accelerating waveguide is applied to input B of the hybrid
coupler. This is through a 3 GHz filter. The filter removes RF signals at unwanted high frequencies
that can occur during some beam conditions.
The RF power through ports A and B of the hybrid coupler decreases by 3 dB at output ports X
and Y. The phase of the X output from the Y output is also 90° out of phase. Each output port
therefore supplies an in phase part and a 90° phase move part of the input signals.
If the signal amplitudes at inputs A and B are equal and in phase, the amplitudes at outputs X and
Y are equal. This is the null point, when the phase error is at zero. A difference between the input
signals occurs, gives a difference in amplitude at outputs X and Y.
Output X and Y from the hybrid coupler are supplied, through 10 dB attenuator pads, to two RF
diodes. These change the signals and apply them to the PPG PCB as signals RF_PHASE_1 and
RF_PHASE_2.
During the center part of the RF pulses, the PPG PCB reads a sample of the phase error signals.
The sample pulses are then compared and applied to the SCC-HTB and AI12-HTB PCBs. Item Item
444 enables the sample procedure.
Signal Description
Phase leg 1 Output PHASE_LEG_1 is a DC voltage level that is in proportion
to the sample value from input RF_PHASE_1. This is supplied to

4.7.8 Operation of the HP phase shifter
This is the operation and analysis of the HP phase shifter.
A servo controls the DC motor that moves the pistons of the HP phase shifter. The motor gets the
feedback from a potentiometer that monitors the positions of the pistons. The movement of the
pistons moves the directional coupler in the waveguide system. This adjusts the phase angle of
the re-cycled RF to align that of the primary RF.
An analog position signal from the potentiometer is applied to the TCS. The TCS selects the
position of the phase shifter as given by the input treatment data. This sends a digital servo
control signal to an AO8 PCB, where it is changed to an analog speed signal. This signal is applied
to a UMD PCB which moves the motor until the potentiometer shows the necessary position.

4.7.8.4 Operation of the HP phase shifter control signal processing
Item Item 162 compares the value that is necessary for the calibration block against the position
of the phase shifter. This is shown by Item 229. Item Item 162 then supplies an 8-bit speed and
direction signal to the AO8-HTA PCB as Item 554. The time of Item 554 is approximately seven
seconds to prevent too high a temperature of the motor.

Product description of the HT and RF systems

The phase shifter control item Item 162 also gives the anti backlash sequence. This makes sure
that the last position of the phase shifter is always from the direction clockwise.
The AO8 HTA PCB changes the digital speed and direction value set by Item 554 to an analog
signal. This is input to UMD PCB 11-6 in the motor drive area as ±5 V signal SET SPEED 1.
Item Item 582 is applied to the ROC-HTA PCB. This PCB has a Darlington pair which supplies an
active low signal MOTOR ENABLE 1. This is the EN input to UMD PCB 11-6. When the EN signal is
set, the drive output of the UMD PCB is input to the motor of the HP phase shifter.
The SET SPEED 1 signal is used by the UMD PCB with the on board signals to set the ratio of the
stable power supply to the DC motor. Microswitch SW1 in the supply line and SW2 in the 0 V line.
This prevents the motor from more than its mechanical limits. Diodes D1 and D2 make the
direction of rotation of the motor opposite if a limit microswitch operates.
4.7.8.5 Operation of the motor position monitor
This is a description of the operation of the motor position monitor.
The DC motor is connected to potentiometer RV1. RV1 is connected between a +10 V supply and
the ground (earth). The wiper of the potentiometer monitors the movement of the motor and
causes the POSITION signal. This is applied to the SCC-HTB and AI12-HTB PCBs. This gives the
position feedback item Item 229.
4.7.8.6 Operation of the motor demand monitor signal processing
This is a description of the operation of the motor demand monitor signal processing.
A sample of the analog version of the speed and position is read by the TCS. Item Item 409 selects
the SET SPEED 1 signal as the output channel of the monitor multiplexer on the AO8-HTA PCB.
This version of the CONTROL signal is applied to the SCC-HTB and AI12-HTB PCBs. This is output as
an 8-bit digital value that is read by the TCS as monitor item Item 39

Troubleshooting for the HT and RF systems
Doing the HT and RF system troubleshooting
5.1 Doing the HT and RF system troubleshooting
This is the recommended procedure to use to do the necessary troubleshooting to correct the
faults that can occur in the HT and RF systems of the linear accelerator.
WARNING 5.1
Do not do work on the digital accelerator without a second person near. The HT and RF systems
have high energy levels which can give you an electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.2
Do not remove a cover from the digital accelerator unless you are qualified and authorized to
do so. If you ignore this warning you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.3
Do not remove the covers or cables from the equipment unless special instructions in this
document tell you to. The covers give access to high voltage electric parts. Always install the
covers and cables again before you use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.4
Do not touch or go near any part of the equipment when the HT is switched on or the circuits
are electrically charged. The HT circuit has voltages of up to 50 kV. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.5
Do not touch or go near the HT or RF systems until the reservoir capacitors are fully discharged.
It is approximately 12 minutes before the capacitors discharge to a safe level. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.6
Do not touch any part of the HT circuit unless you switch off all electrical power. Use the HT
discharge rod to fully discharge high voltage capacitors. If you ignore this waning, you can cause
fatal injury.
WARNING 5.7
Do not work on the RF isolator unless you wear the applicable protective equipment (face
masks and gloves). The RF isolator contains Beryllia. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
serious injury.
1 Put the free end of the safety discharge rod on the anode (positive terminal (+)) of diode D1
of the supply transformer.
2 Let the capacitors fully discharge for a minimum of 12 minutes.
3 When the PFN is discharged, remove the HT safety discharge rod from the anode of diode
D1.

5.1.1 Doing a test of the HT PSU voltage
This is the procedure to do a test to make sure that the voltage of the HT power supply unit (PSU)
is 600 V.
Prerequisites
Digital voltmeter (DVM)
WARNING 5.8
Do not do work on the digital accelerator without a second person near. The HT and RF systems
have high energy levels which can give you an electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.9
Do not remove a cover from the digital accelerator unless you are qualified and authorized to
do so. If you ignore this warning you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.10
Do not remove the covers or cables from the equipment unless special instructions in this
document tell you to. The covers give access to high voltage electric parts. Always install the
covers and cables again before you use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.11
Do not touch or go near any part of the equipment when the HT is switched on or the circuits
are electrically charged. The HT circuit has voltages of up to 50 kV. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.12
Do not touch or go near the HT or RF systems until the reservoir capacitors are fully discharged.
It is approximately 12 minutes before the capacitors discharge to a safe level. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 5.13

Troubleshooting for the HT and RF systems
Doing the troubleshooting of the pulse forming network
5.2.1 Doing a test of the thyratron trigger pulse and cables
This is the procedure to do a test of the thyratron trigger pulse and cables.
Prerequisites
Oscilloscope
WARNING 5.28
Do not work in the modulator area with the modulator cover removed unless you wear a face
shield, a protective jacket, and protective gloves. If the diode D1 fails, shards of ceramic
material and dielectric oil may be ejected. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious
injury.
1 At the service panel, connect an oscilloscope to the THY G2 test point
2 Examine the waveform on the oscilloscope, make sure that the PFN charges and discharges
to 4 V or 8 V.
Note: It is possible to do a check of the THY G2 signal if the equipment room door is open.
3 If the PFN does not charge and discharge correctly, interchange the fiber-optic cable for a
replacement that you know is serviceable.
4 Make sure that there are no warnings on the screen, for example, HT overtemperature.
5.2.2 Doing a test of the voltages of the pulse forming network
This is the procedure to do a test of the voltages of the pulse forming network (PFN) of the HT
system of the digital accelerator.
Prerequisites
Oscilloscope
WARNING 5.29
Do not work in the modulator area with the modulator cover removed unless you wear a face
shield, a protective jacket, and protective gloves. If the diode D1 fails, shards of ceramic
material and dielectric oil may be ejected. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious
injury.
1 At the service panel connect an oscilloscope to the PFN V test point.
2 Measure the signal.
3 At the TCS, make sure that the status of Item 88 is 1 (charge enable).
4 Do a check of the HE fiber-optic cable of the PFN.
5 If it is necessary, interchange the HE fiber-optic cable for a replacement that you know is
serviceable.
6 Do steps 1 to 3 for the low power mode.
7 Do a check of the LE fiber-optic cable of the PFN.
8 If it is necessary, interchange the LE fiber-optic cable for a replacement that you know is
serviceable.

5.9 Testing diodes (Method A)
1 Disconnect the two BNCs from the diode block.
2 Remove the two screws that fix the diode block to the phase error detector chassis.
3 Unscrew the two SMA connectors on the attenuators from the diode block.
4 Turn the diode block over and reconnect the attenuators and the BNC connectors, PLD to
SKF and PLF to SKD.
5 Refit the two screws that hold the block to the chassis.

Testing attenuators (Method B)
5.10 Testing attenuators (Method B)
1 Disconnect the two BNCs from the diode block.
2 Remove the two screws that fix the diode block to the phase error detector chassis.
3 Unscrew the two SMA connectors on the attenuators from the diode block.
4 Unscrew the two SMA connectors, SKC and SKE, from the hybrid coupler.
5 Swap the attenuators and reconnect to SKC and SKE.
6 Reconnect the attenuators to the diode block.
7 Refit the two screws that hold the block to the chassis.
5.11 Probe isolation check (Method C)
1 Carefully disconnect the semi-rigid coaxial cable from the RF probe.
2 Using a DMM, check the resistance between the probe and an unpainted part of the
waveguide.
3 If the resistance is low, then check that there is a piece of insulating plastic around the
probe shaft between it and the socket.
5.12 Testing the hybrid coupler (Method D)
1 Disconnect the 3 GHz filter and the semi-rigid cable from the LP phase shifter from the
hybrid coupler.
2 Remove the two screws (in the centre of the hybrid coupler) that hold the hybrid coupler to
the spacer plate.
3 Unscrew the two attenuators from SKC and SKE.
4 Turn the hybrid coupler face down keeping the outputs towards the attenuators.
5 Reconnect the attenuators, replace the two screws and reconnect the filter and semi-rigid
from the LP phase shifter.

5.13.4 Method D
1 Use a multimeter in Ohmmeter mode to test a thyristor.
a Connect the positive output lead of the multimeter to the anode and the negative
lead to the cathode.
b Make sure that the multimeter shows no continuity.
2 Short circuit the gate of the thyristor to the anode. Make sure that the multimeter shows
continuity through the thyristor.
Note: When the gate short circuit is removed from the anode, conduction can stop or continue if the
multimeter supplies enough current to keep the device above its holding current level.
Note: If the multimeter shows continuity through the thyristor before the gate is touched to the anode,
it shows that the thyristor is shorted.
Note: If the multimeter will not show continuity through the thyristor after the gate was touched to the
anode, it shows that the thyristor is open circuit.
5.13.5 Method E
The control PCBs that follow are all equivalent:
• The single phase supplies
• Magnetron Magnet (PCB 13D)
• Focus 2 (PCB 13E)

degrees (6.67 mS).

Service Parts Repair of the HT and RF systems
Checking the thyratron grid voltages
6.1 Checking the thyratron grid voltages
Before you remove a thyratron, do the following checks:
1 Measure the voltages on the wires according to the following table and make sure that the
voltage is within specification.
Table 6.1 Thyratron grid voltages
Supply Wire Limits
Replenisher voltage Red 5.0 V AC to 6.5 V AC
Heater Yellow 6.0 V AC to 6.6 V AC
Grid 2 Pink -120 V DC to -160 V DC
Grid 1 Green 16.5 V DC to 22 V DC
2 Adjust the voltages if necessary.
Note: The thyratron can be damaged by operation at the incorrect settings and in a short time become
unserviceable.
6.2 Removing the thyratron
This is the procedure to remove the thyratron from the modulator assembly in Area 30 of the
linear accelerator.
The thyratron is installed behind the rectifier unit of the magnetron filament.
WARNING 6.1
Do not do work on the linear accelerator without a second person near. The HT and RF systems
have high energy levels which can give you an electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.2
Do not do work on the high voltages areas of the system, until you switch off the HT circuit
breaker and discharge the charge circuit. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.3
Do not touch or go near the HT or RF systems until the reservoir capacitors are fully discharged.
It is approximately 12 minutes before the capacitors discharge to a safe level. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.4
Do not work on the equipment if the gantry can move or is out of balance. Always attach the
gantry locking straps when you do work on the equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
1 Rotate the gantry to approximately 315° (-45°).
2 Lock the gantry.

The Select Machine State dialog box appears.
4 Click Not Initialized.
This sets the linear accelerator to the Not Initialized machine state.
5 Disconnect the electrical supply to the linear accelerator.
WARNING 6.5
Do not work in the modulator area with the modulator cover removed unless you wear a face
shield, a protective jacket, and protective gloves. If the diode D1 fails, shards of ceramic
material and dielectric oil may be ejected. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious
injury.
6 Remove the cover of the modulator assembly.
7 Put the free end of the HT safety discharge rod on the anode terminal (D1+VE) of the diode
D1 on the transformer (see (5) in Figure 6.1).

6.3 Installing the thyratron
This is the procedure to install the thyratron in the modulator assembly in Area 30 of the linear
accelerator.
WARNING 6.7
Do not work on the linear accelerator without a second person near. The HT and RF systems
have high energy levels which can give you an electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.8
Do not touch or go near any part of the equipment when the HT is switched on or the circuits
are electrically charged. The HT circuit has voltages of up to 50 kV. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.9
Do not work on the high voltages areas of the system, until you switch off the HT circuit breaker
and discharge the charge circuit. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.10
Do not touch or go near the HT or RF systems until the reservoir capacitors are fully discharged.
It is approximately 12 minutes before the capacitors discharge to a safe level. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.11
Do not operate the linear accelerator in clinical mode until you do the applicable test, set up,
and calibration procedures for the replacement parts. Record the part numbers of the
replacement parts when you replace a part. If you ignore this warning, you can cause clinical
mistreatment.

© Elekta Group 2022. All rights reserved. Page 155

Service Parts Repair of the HT and RF systems
Removing the electron gun
WARNING 6.12
Do not work in the modulator area with the modulator cover removed unless you wear a face
shield, a protective jacket, and protective gloves. If the diode D1 fails, shards of ceramic
material and dielectric oil may be ejected. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious
injury.
1 Put the thyratron in position on the chassis of the modulator assembly. Make sure that the
wires do not catch the axial fan.
2 Install the four screws to attach the thyratron to the chassis.
3 Install the three flying leads on the studs, and tighten the nuts:
– Red (reservoir)
– Green (grid 1)
– Yellow (cathode).
4 Tighten the grid 2 connection on the thyratron flange.
5 Connect the thyratron inductor coil.
6 Put the thyratron access cover in position and install the eight screws.
7 Install the top cover of the modulator assembly.
8 Connect the electrical supply to the linear accelerator.
9 Prepare the linear accelerator for operation.
10 Do a check of the thyratron conditions of operation.
6.4 Removing the electron gun
Prerequisites
Special tools and equipment
• 3 mm hexagonal head key
• Dry nitrogen supply
• Turbo molecular vacuum pump
• Lint-free cloth
• Nylon gloves
• Filter paper
• Solvent (pure isopropyl alcohol)
• Vacuum purge and vent tool kit 1020015.
The electron gun has a set life, and therefore it is necessary to interchange the gun filament at
times. Elekta recommends that you keep a replacement electron gun as a service part, and keep it
in a vacuum until you install it. The vacuum helps to decrease the effects of outgassing after its
installation when you start the linear accelerator.
WARNING 6.13

WARNING 6.46
Do not work on the RF isolator unless you wear the applicable protective equipment (face maks
and gloves). The RF isolator contains Beryllia. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious
injury.
WARNING 6.47
Do not remove material that is contaminated with beryllia without the applicable protective
equipment (face mask and gloves). The dust from beryllia can be hazardous to health. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Note: If you must remove an item contaminated with beryllia, put the item in a suitable bag, seal it up,
then apply an appropriate warning label on the bag.

6

WARNING 6.49

irritated. There can be preferential breakdown of the sulfurhexafloride (SF ). If you ignore this
6
warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.50
Do not work on the RF system without wearing gloves. There can be preferential breakdown of
the sulfurhexafloride (SF ). If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.

6.18 Mechanical check after potentiometer replacement
This is a procedure to check the LP phase shifter movement after replacing the potentiometer in
the phase error detector assembly.
1 Switch on the electrical supply to the linear accelerator.
2 Log in to Service Mode.
3 Click Service Functions.
4 Set the linear accelerator to the Preparatory machine state.
5 Load the Service Pages and click the Defaults button.
6 On the AFC service page, change the part 1 value of item 163 LP Ph. ctrl to a value of 15.00.
– Make sure that the part 1 value changes to be the same as the part 4 value.
– Change the part 1 value to 36.00 and make sure that the part 4 value changes to be
the same as the part 1 value.
– Change the part 1 value to 3.00 and make sure that the part 4 value changes to be the
same as the part 1 value.
Note: After the mechanical checks are complete, follow the Restoring the AFC settings procedure.

Doing a check of the hp phase shifter operation (for feedback machines only)
7.1 Doing a check of the hp phase shifter operation (for
feedback machines only)
1 Use the Calibrate Function, select Cal Item 1 to Item 429, HP Phase position.
2 Set Control Item to Item 362 and type a Control Item Value of 2.00.
a Make sure that the hp phase shifter is not in a limit condition.
b If necessary increase the Control Value.
3 Read the actual position of the hp phase shifter through the scale window and type this
value into the Cal Value.
4 Type a Control Value of 14.00 and make sure that the hp phase shifter is not in a limit
condition. Change the control value if it is in a limit condition.
5 Type the actual scale reading into the Cal. Value.
6 Do steps 2 to 5 again until the readout of the hp phase position, Item 429, is the same as
the actual reading at both ends of the scale.
7 De-select Cal Item 1 by deleting and entering and then SAVE the Database.
Note: All other related calibration is done automatically.
8 Make sure that the hp phase shifter operates over a minimum range of 15 cm and that the
mechanical scale and readout agree to ± 0.5 cm.

7.3.4 Operation of the gun warm up
With the NULL energy cal block loaded, after 30 minutes the gun current is increased to the value
set in Item 601 part 1 for 15 minutes. During the initial 15 minute warm up timers, the gun
current is also increased to this Item 601 part 1 level.
This function was included to help users with machines, that had cold start-up problems. It
greatly increases the gun filament wear out though. The value of part 1 of item 601 should be the
same as the gun standby for Xrays and should only be increased on machines that have severe
cold start problems.

a Set Item 380 Gun stby I to the value 5.25 A ±0.25 A.
b Click Save Energy Cal. Blocks.
7 Select all of the available Electron calibration blocks in turn.
8 In each calibration block, do the steps that follow.
a Set Item 380 Gun stby I to the value 4.00 A ±0.50 A.
b Click Save Energy Cal. Blocks.

7.4.2 Making a plot of the electron gun curve
This is the procedure to make a plot of the electron gun curve which calculates the capture range
of the gun servo for a selected energy.
1 Log on to Service Mode.
2 Click Display Service Pages.
The Display Service Pages dialog box appears.
3 Load the Beam Control service page.
4 Load the Beam Monitor service page.
5 Make sure that the value of Item 304 Bending C is a minimum of 98% of the peak output.
6 Set Item 327 Gun I ctrl to 0 (Manual).
7 Make a plot of the value of Item 327 Gun I ctrl against Item 546 Gun Diff for the nominal
bending current.
8 Make a plot at +10% of the energy.
For example, if 10 MV = 61 A, then +10% = 67.1 A.
9 Make a plot at -10% of the energy.
For example, if 10 MV = 61 A, then -10% = 54.9 A.

7.6 Calibrating the thyratron grid monitor
This is the procedure to calibrate the thyratron grid monitor of the linear accelerator.
The modulator isolation PCB controls the thyratron grid monitor. The grid monitor receives an
input from the thyratron pulser unit. The input is a DC voltage in proportion to the voltage on the
thyratron G1 grid. The grid monitor sends outputs through a fiber-optic driver.
When the thyratron G1 grid is at the correct voltage for operation, the lamp of the fiber-optic
driver comes on. This is related to a start value of approximately 4062 in the off condition. This
value changes to approximately 100 in the on condition.
1 Log on to Service Mode.
2 Click Select Machine States.
3 In the Select Machine States dialog box, set the linear accelerator to the Preparatory
machine state.
4 Let the Thyratron Timer inhibit clear.
5 Click Edit Machine Item Part.
The Edit Machine Item Parts dialog box appears.
6 Set the parts of Item 220 Thy Grid to the values in Table 7.6.
Table 7.6 Part values of Item 220 (thyratron grid monitor)
Part Value
10 0
11 2048
12 2048
121 300
122 10

overload. If you ignore this caution, it can cause damage to the equipment.

7.7.5.2 Doing a test of the bending magnet current
This is the procedure to test the bending magnet current of the waveguide of the linear
accelerator.
1 Configure the power conditions.
2 Set the X Plot Item to Item 304 Bending C.
3 Type 1024 into X Gain.
4 Set the Plotter X Channel to 2 V/cm.
5 Set the Y1 Plot Item to Item 484 Plates sum.
6 Type 90 into Y Gain to 90.
7 Set the Plotter Y Channel to 20 V/cm.
8 Make a plot of Item 484 Plates sum against Item 304 Bending C for adjustments of Item
304 Bending C and Item 305 Bending F.
In the range of the graph, the dose rate drops immediately to less than 50% in each
direction.
9 Make sure that the difference between the selected value of Item 304 Bending C and the
maximum dose rate curve is less than 5%. For 25 MV, the difference must be less than 10%.
Note: If power conditions subsequently change, do a check of the bending currents again.
10 Do steps 2 to 9 again for all X-ray energies.

7.7.6.5 Doing an HT overload test
This is the procedure to do an HT overload test for the linear accelerator.
WARNING 7.35
Do not do work on the linear accelerator without a second person near. The HT and RF systems
have high energy levels which can give you an electric shock. If you ignore this warning, it can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 7.36
Do not do work on the high voltages areas of the system, until you switch off the HT circuit
breaker and discharge the charge circuit. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 7.37
Do not touch or go near any part of the equipment when the HT is switched on or the circuits
are electrically charged. The HT circuit has voltages of up to 50 kV. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 7.38
Do not touch or go near the HT or RF systems until the reservoir capacitors are fully discharged.
It is approximately 12 minutes before the capacitors discharge to a safe level. If you ignore this
warning, it can cause fatal injury.

7.8 RF system calibration and tests
This is a description of the test and setup of the RF system.
Before you do the set up of the RF system, it is important to adjust the HP phase shifter to the
approximate position.
When do the AFC system set up, you must set the beam frequency to that which gives the
maximum output. This frequency is referred to as fo and has a nominal value of 2858 MHz. It is
necessary to find the beam frequency before you do the RF system checks.

7.8.1.2 Configuring the RF probes
This is the procedure to configure the RF probes of the magnetron tuner servo of the linear
accelerator.
Note: When setting up the AFC System, it is essential that the frequency used is that which gives the
maximum output (f ).
o
1 Select Xlow with the wedge out and 29980 monitor units.
2 Set the machine to operate at half maximum PRF.
3 Set the field shape to Patient Plane Leakage.
4 Set Interlock Group 8, confirm the beam and start the machine.
5 Ensure the AFC runs in auto; does the tuner drive off; if so rotate the rear probe by 180˚
ensuring you maintain its perpendicular position to the RF field.
6 Rotate the gantry to +90° or -90° to gain access to the LP phase shifter.
7 Run the machine with the tuner in auto and gun at maximum output. Allow machine to
warmup and settle the tuner position. This will be f .
o
8 Set the tuner to manual.
9 Set the gun to 5.00A.
10 Interrupt the machine and disconnect the gun end semi-rigid co-axial cables from the LP
phase shifter assembly then fit a 50Ω load on to the LP phase shifter.
11 Run the machine.
Note: The tuner should still be at f . If not, set the tuner correctly. Note the values of Phase Leg 1 and 2.
o
12 Adjust the centre joint probe rotation as necessary using a suitable tool to achieve the
desired phase leg values of 9 ±1 (8 to 9 for feedback). The maximum difference between
phase leg of 1 and 2 cannot be more than 15%. If greater than 15%, then swap the RF
diodes to get a better match.
13 Reconnect the semi rigid coaxial cable. Run the machine with the AFC system in auto and
the gun at maximum output.
14 Adjust the LP phase shifter position so that the tuner is on maximum output.
15 Adjust the rear probe by adjusting the height not rotating until phase leg 1 and 2 are
between 10 and 11 (8.5 to 9.5 for feedback machines).

7.8.4 Setting the AFC for linear accelerators without feedback
In linear accelerators without feedback, the frequency optimization changes from that of linear
accelerators with feedback. The power conditions and bending currents are different from those in
a feedback digital accelerators.
1 Log on to Service Mode.

7.8.7 Checking the HP phase shifter position for FFF (feedback only)
This is the procedure to adjust the HP phase shifter for changes in frequency and gun current for
feedback machines.
Note:
You must make a note of the changes to the flatness, energy, stability and gun servo as any
change can require re-work to the beam setup procedure.
When the HP phase position is selected, make sure that the automatic frequency control (AFC) can
maintain a stable output at the maximum achievable dose rate.
For FFF energies, the HP Phase position is copied over from the 6 or 10 MV flat energies and in
most cases will not require adjustment. When the current position satisfies this condition, Elekta
recommends that you make no adjustments. If during the setup procedure, the HP phase position
is changed, it will change the flatness, energy, stability, and the gun servo operation.
1 Do the following steps to check the HP Phase shifter position:
a On the Gun Servo service page, set Item 181 Gun Auto? to Manual.
b Adjust Item 327 Gun l ctrl for maximum dose rate.
c Use a graphing tool to plot Item 161 Tuner Position against Item 484 Plates sum for
the chosen HP Phase position.
d Adjust Item 163 LP phase control to bring the tuner position close to the tuner rest
position.
e Adjust Item 163 LP phase control in increments of 0.1 to control the tuner position on
the graph past the maximum dose rate operating position.
f If the HP Phase position provide a stable output and a flat peak operating position
proceed to step 3 .
g If the HP Phase position require adjustment proceed with step 2 and 3 .
2 If required adjust Item 162 HP ph.ctrl in increments of 0.5, and repeat the plot as described
step 1.
3 Select the HP phase shifter setting that gives sufficient dose rate with a stable flat peak.
4 To validate the chosen HP Phase position, proceed as follows:
a Set Item 327 Gun l ctrl to 20 mA above the maximum dose rate gun value.
b Make a plot of Item 484 Ion Plate Sum against Item 161 Tuner Position.
c Set Item 327 Gun l ctrl to 20 mA below the maximum dose rate gun value.
d Make a plot of Item 484 Ion Plate Sum against Item 161 Tuner Position.
e Make sure that the selected value of Item 162 HP ph.ctrl gives:

Note: It may be necessary to use smaller increment of 0.1 to optimize the HP Phase position.
5 For the chosen HP Phase position, proceed as follows:
a Return Item 163 LP phase ctrl to Auto and make sure that the linear accelerator
operates at maximum dose rate.
b Set Item 229 HP ph.posn part 1 to the same value as part 4.
c Return Item 181 Gun Auto? to Auto.

7.9 Doing a check of the HT and RF interlocks
7.9.1 Doing a test of the vacuum/gun filament interlock for machines
from serial number 151553 with Varian ion pumps
This is the procedure to do a test of the vacuum/gun filament interlock. This task is only applicable
for linear accelerators from serial number 151553 with Varian ion pumps.
1 Make sure that the part 10 value of Item 487 10-4 set G and Item 492 10-4 set T is set to
40.
2 Load the Gun Servo service page.
3 Do a check of the brightness of the gun filament, this is a dim red.
4 In HTCA AREA 16, make sure that LED 9 on the ROC-HTB PCB is on.
5 In AREA 18 on the front of the IPCU, set the TARGET channel switch to OFF. Make sure that
the GUN channel switch stays in the ON position.
6 Make sure that the gun filament voltage does not change and that LED 9 on the ROC-HTB
PCB stays on.
7 Make sure that the ‘Target’ 10-4 vacuum inhibit Item 239 10-4 trp T is displayed.
8 On the front of the IPCU, set the TARGET channel switch to ON.
9 Make sure that the the ‘Target’ 10-4 vacuum inhibit Item 239 10-4 trp T clears.
10 On the front of the IPCU, set the GUN channel switch to OFF.
11 Make sure that the gun filament is off, and that you cannot see signs of brightness when
you look at the gun filament.
This occurs approximately three seconds after you set the GUN channel switch to OFF.
12 On the ROC-HTB PCB, make sure that LED 9 is off.
13 Make sure that, the ‘Gun’ 10-4 vacuum inhibit Item 238 10-4 trp G is displayed.
14 On the front of the IPCU, set the GUN channel switch to ON.
15 Make sure that the ‘Gun’ 10-4 vacuum inhibit Item 238 10-4 trp G clears.
16 Make sure that the gun filament is a dim red to show that it is on. This occurs approximately
40 seconds after you set the GUN channel switch to ON.
17 On the ROC-HTB PCB, make sure that LED 9 is on.

238 10-4 trp G are displayed.
20 On the rear panel of the IPCU, put the 2 mm CABLE INTERLOCK jack plug in the TARGET
port.
21 On the IPCU, set the power switch to off and then to reset.
22 On the rear panel of the IPCU, remove the 2 mm CABLE INTERLOCK jack plug from the GUN

23 Make sure that the gun filament is off, and that you cannot see signs of brightness when

This occurs approximately three seconds after you remove the 2 mm CABLE INTERLOCK jack

7.9.2 Doing a test of the interlock of the LP phase shifter
This is the procedure to do a test of the operation of the interlock of the LP phase shifter.
1 Log on to Service Mode.
2 Click Edit Machine Item Parts.
The Edit Machine Item Parts dialog box appears.
3 Record the values of Item 163 LP ph.ctrl, Part 19 and Part 20.
4 Type 4000 in Item 163 LP ph.ctrl, Part 19.
5 Type 0 in Item 163 LP ph.ctrl, Part 20.
6 Load the Tuning/AFC service page.
7 Type 0.0 in Item 163 LP ph.ctrl.
8 Make sure that the operation of the first limit switches, stops the movement of the LP
phase shifter.
9 Type 40.0 in Item 163 LP ph.ctrl.
10 Make sure that the operation of the second limit switch stops the movement of the LP
phase shifter.
11 Type the value 25.0 in Item 163 LP ph.ctrl.
The system gives no display of the scale, it is therefore not necessary to do calibration.
12 Set Item 163 LP ph.ctrl, Part 19 and Part 20 to the values recorded in step 3 .
13 Make sure that the LP phase shifter operates in the range of 2 units to 37 units.
